Personally this interface excites me more than collections like Dask.dataframe; there is a lot of freedom in arbitrary task submission.
If you're excited about machine learning you can stop reading here. However, if you're interested in how to build distributed algorithms with Dask then you may want to read on.
posts.key_insight: Distributed computing frameworks provide powerful primitives for building custom algorithms, but computational power cannot compensate for poor problem understanding—domain expertise matters more than scale.

posts.summary: This technical post, written by Jim Crist and hosted on Matt Rocklin's blog, introduces dask-learn, an experimental library combining Dask with scikit-learn for parallelized machine learning. The post demonstrates how Dask's implementation of GridSearchCV achieves ~4X speedup over scikit-learn's joblib-based version by avoiding redundant computations through intelligent hashing and graph-based execution. The key insight is that when pipelines have multiple stages, Dask shares intermediate results rather than recomputing them for every parameter combination. The post also shows how the same code can run locally or distributed with minimal changes, thanks to Dask's scheduler abstraction.
key_points: Dask-learn achieves 4X speedup on GridSearchCV by avoiding duplicate work through Merkle DAG-style hashing of intermediate results
The approach is most beneficial when pipelines have expensive repeated steps - single estimators or cheap operations show little improvement over joblib
Dask's scheduler abstraction allows the same grid search code to run locally (threaded) or distributed with just a few lines changed
The implementation uses dask.delayed for readable code and handles arbitrary graph topologies beyond simple map-reduce patterns
Design tradeoffs include making estimators immutable (requiring est = est.fit(X, y)) while GridSearchCV executes immediately due to the refit step
money_quotes: The reason is simply that the dask version is doing less work.
While embarassingly parallel, this can also result in repeated work, as earlier stages in the pipeline are refit multiple times on the same parameter + data combinations.
Due to the internal hashing used in dask (which is extensible!), duplicate computations are avoided.
Since the graphs are separated from the scheduler, this works both locally and distributed with only a few extra lines.
The approach presented here is nice, but is really only beneficial when there's duplicate work to be avoided, and that duplicate work is expensive.
I am not a machine learning expert. Do not consider this a guide on how to do machine learning, the usage of scikit-learn below is probably naive.
posts.key_insight: Dask's graph-based execution model can significantly outperform embarrassingly parallel approaches by identifying and eliminating redundant computations through intelligent hashing of intermediate results.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin addresses the gap between individual and institutional adoption of Dask, documenting common questions from universities, research labs, and companies trying to deploy Dask at scale. The post covers integration with existing job schedulers (SGE/LSF/Mesos), dynamic cluster scaling, software environment management, security concerns, scheduler resilience, and multi-datacenter deployment. Rocklin is refreshingly honest about Dask's limitations—particularly its complete lack of security and single-point-of-failure scheduler—while outlining potential solutions. He positions Dask as an 'all-terrain vehicle' for exploratory computing, distinct from database 'trains' and Spark 'automobiles,' serving users whose problems are too complex and heterogeneous for traditional big data tools.
key_points: Institutions consistently struggle with underutilized clusters because existing job schedulers don't support the interactive, dynamic workloads that Python users need
Dask is 'incredibly insecure' by design, relying on external tools like Docker and network controls for security rather than building it in
The scheduler is a single point of failure with no backup mechanism, though clients can reconnect and resubmit after scheduler recovery
Dask targets a different niche than Spark: complex, heterogeneous, exploratory computing rather than ETL and database-like operations
Adoption is happening bottom-up, with individuals and small groups pushing interest up the organizational stack
money_quotes: Dask.distributed is incredibly insecure. It allows anyone with network access to the scheduler to execute arbitrary code in an unprotected environment.
I hesitate to jump into providing 'just a little bit of security' without going all the way for fear of providing a false sense of security.
You really should never trust an author of a project to give a fair and unbiased view of the capabilities of the tools in the surrounding landscape.
Dask is like an all-terrain-vehicle: it takes you out of town on rough ground that hasn't been properly explored before.
It turned out to be a harder problem than expected to design/build/maintain such a system in-house.

The people I talk to are people for whom Spark/Databases are clearly not an appropriate fit. They are tackling problems that are way more complex, more heterogeneous, and with a broader variety of users.
Culturally Dask has managed to simultaneously cater to both the open science crowd as well as the private-sector crowd.
posts.key_insight: Institutional adoption of distributed Python computing is held back not by the core technology but by the surrounding infrastructure—job scheduler integration, security, and resilience—which open source projects must solve collaboratively rather than leaving each institution to build custom solutions.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin explores the social dynamics between open source developers and users seeking help, distinguishing between 'veteran users' who skillfully distill problems into reproducible examples and 'novice users' who require more hand-holding. He argues that creating minimal reproducible examples is arguably more valuable than fixing the actual bugs, as it enables developers to quickly understand issues without diving into unfamiliar domains. Rocklin acknowledges the tension between the traditional expectation that open source developers freely help users and the modern reality of professional developers with limited time. He suggests that the community needs to make it easier for developers to decline requests while also elevating user skills through public recognition of 'veteran' behaviors.
key_points: Creating minimal, reproducible bug reports is arguably more important than fixing the bugs themselves because it allows developers to quickly understand problems without domain knowledge
There's a cultural mismatch between users expecting free help (based on the old 'basement hacker' stereotype) and modern professional developers who charge hundreds per hour for consulting
Open source projects can scale support through broadcast solutions like StackOverflow and documentation rather than individual hand-holding
Being direct with technically experienced novices about expectations (like requesting MCVEs) works surprisingly well because they understand that others' time is valuable
The community should honor and publicly praise 'veteran user' skills to encourage others to improve how they report issues and ask questions
money_quotes: These distilled reports are arguably more important than fixing the actual bug or writing the actual documentation.

Knowing what to do and how to ask for help is hard. Having the guts to walk into a chat room where people will quickly see that you're a novice is even harder. It's like using public transit in a deeply foreign language.

We now have paid professionals building software that is used by thousands or millions of users. These professionals easily charge consulting fees of hundreds of dollars per hour for exactly the kind of assistance that people show up expecting for free under the previous model.
I don't know how to say 'I'm sorry, I simply don't have the time to help you with your problem.' in a way that feels at all compassionate.
I think that we need to foster an environment in which developers can say 'Sorry. Busy.' more easily.

My hope is that by attaching the language of skill, like the term 'veteran', to user behaviors we can create an environment where people are proud of how cleanly they can raise issues and how clearly they can describe questions for documentation.
posts.key_insight: The open source community needs to both normalize developers declining help requests and elevate 'veteran user' skills as a point of pride, recognizing that well-crafted bug reports and questions are as valuable as the code fixes themselves.

posts.summary: This release announcement for Dask Distributed 1.13.0 details major user-facing features including renaming Executor to Client for clarity, worker memory spill-to-disk capability, and improved Bokeh visualizations. The post also introduces experimental features like publishing datasets for collaboration, task-launched tasks for dynamic workloads, and live environment redeployment. Internal improvements include a state-machine scheduler architecture, connection pooling, and enhanced work stealing. The release represents significant progress in both usability and robustness for distributed computing in Python.
key_points: Executor renamed to Client to avoid confusion with Spark/Mesos terminology where 'executor' means something different
Workers can now spill excess data to disk when memory runs low, improving robustness for large problems on small clusters
New experimental publish_dataset feature allows sharing computed results between collaborators without re-running computations
Tasks can now launch other tasks, enabling highly dynamic workloads that adapt based on computed values
Workers can restart in new Conda environments live, enabling faster experimentation than Docker deployment cycles
money_quotes: Unfortunately, this is the same term used by projects like Spark and Mesos for 'the low-level thing that executes tasks on each of the workers' causing significant confusion when communicating with other communities or for transitioning users.
Generally this feature should improve robustness and allow the solution of larger problems on smaller clusters, although with a performance cost.
Dask's policies to reduce memory use through clever scheduling remain in place, so in the common case you should never need this feature, but it's nice to have as a failsafe.

You can now submit tasks to the cluster that themselves submit more tasks. This allows the submission of highly dynamic workloads that can shape themselves depending on future computed values without ever checking back in with the original client.

This helps users to experiment with different software environments with a much faster turnaround time (typically tens of seconds) than asking IT to install libraries or building and deploying Docker containers.
posts.key_insight: Distributed systems benefit from both robustness features like disk spilling that handle failure gracefully, and flexibility features like dynamic task launching that let computations evolve without returning to the client.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin reflects on the tradeoff between writing blog posts and documentation for software projects. Blog posts excel at generating interest and spreading the word about new features, but they quickly become stale and confusing as software evolves. Documentation provides better user comprehension and engagement but lacks the viral distribution channels that blogs enjoy. He concludes that core developers should spend 80% of their prose time on documentation, while reserving blogging for concepts and experiences rather than feature how-tos.
key_points: Blog posts generate interest and spread virally but create technical debt as software evolves and examples break
Documentation improves user comprehension and shows better engagement metrics (longer page views, deeper navigation) but lacks distribution channels
No one subscribes to documentation updates, so even great docs struggle to reach users the way blogs do
The recommended balance is 80% documentation, 20% blogging about concepts and experiences rather than features
Stable documentation pages can potentially be polished into blog posts once features have matured
money_quotes: Code is only as good as its prose.
Like many programmers I spend more time writing prose than code.
Blogging about evolving software also generates debt.
Documentation gets updated as features mature. New pages assimilate cleanly and obsolete pages get cleaned up.
No one subscribes to receive documentation updates.

Blogposts are way sexier. This feels a little shallow if you're not into sales and marketing, but lets remember that software dies without users and that users are busy people who have to be stimulated into taking the time to learn new things.
I'll probably write about the experiences of a developer meeting Mesos for the first time, but I probably won't include a how-to of using Dask with Mesos.
posts.key_insight: Software documentation and blog posts serve different purposes—docs for comprehension and longevity, blogs for discovery and excitement—and developers must consciously balance both to serve users well.

posts.summary: This post compares Dask.distributed and Celery for distributed task processing in Python. The biggest architectural difference is that Dask workers retain intermediate results and communicate directly with each other, while Celery routes all results through a central broker. Through benchmarks and feature comparisons including dependency management, queues, retrying, and rate limiting, Rocklin shows Dask can handle Celery-style workloads but lacks some of Celery's battle-tested convenience features. He acknowledges his bias toward Dask while concluding that Celery's deep API evolved from solving real production problems repeatedly.
key_points: Dask workers hold intermediate results and communicate peer-to-peer, while Celery/Airflow/Luigi route everything through central storage - this architectural difference was why Dask wasn't built on top of existing task systems
Celery requires pre-registering functions on servers (more secure), while Dask lets users run arbitrary code from the client side (better for exploration)
Dask shows significantly lower latency (20ms vs 500ms roundtrip) and lower overhead in benchmarks, which matters for interactive applications
Celery has rich built-in features like task retrying and rate limiting that Dask lacks, forcing users to implement workarounds
Dask's approach of allowing futures within submit calls eliminates the need for complex workflow primitives like Celery's Canvas vocabulary
money_quotes: Dask workers hold onto intermediate results and communicate data between each other while in Celery all results flow back to a central authority.
Computational systems like Dask do this, more data-engineering systems like Celery/Airflow/Luigi don't. This is the main reason why Dask wasn't built on top of Celery/Airflow/Luigi originally.

That's not a knock against Celery/Airflow/Luigi by any means. Typically they're used in settings where this doesn't matter and they've focused their energies on several features that Dask similarly doesn't care about or do well.

In Celery we depend on other widely deployed systems like RabbitMQ or Redis. Dask depends on lower-level Tornado TCP IOStreams and Dask's own custom routing logic. This makes Dask trivial to set up, but also probably less durable.
Dask's trick of allowing futures in submit calls actually goes pretty far. Dask doesn't really need any additional primitives.
Celery evolved in this domain and developed tons of features that solve problems that arise over and over again. This history saves users an enormous amount of time.
Many of Dask's tricks are general enough that they can solve Celery problems with a small bit of effort, but there's still that extra step.
posts.key_insight: Dask can handle Celery-style workloads with better performance and simpler dependency syntax, but Celery's years of evolution in production environments produced convenience features that Dask users must implement themselves.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin addresses the challenge of deploying Dask across diverse cluster resource managers like Kubernetes, SLURM, and Mesos. He acknowledges that while power users can successfully deploy Dask by reading documentation, the barrier remains too high for non-power users. The post explores two potential solutions: maintaining a registry of community-contributed deployment examples, and developing clean API boundaries that separate deployment policies from backend implementations. As a demonstration, he built an adaptive deployment system for Marathon that separates the 'when to scale' logic from the 'how to scale' implementation, allowing either component to be replaced independently.
key_points: Dask is already deployed on numerous cluster systems (SGE, SLURM, Kubernetes, Mesos, etc.) but primarily by power users who can navigate the complexity themselves
The core challenge is supporting many resource managers with different scaling policies while keeping Dask's concerns separable from deployment configuration
A clean API boundary between adaptive scaling logic and backend-specific deployment code enables composable, replaceable components
The proposed Adaptive class handles when to scale (policy) while backend-specific classes like MarathonCluster handle how to scale (implementation)
Community-driven registries of deployment solutions may be the pragmatic answer, supporting copy-paste-edit workflows rather than one-size-fits-all solutions
money_quotes: These systems provide users access to cluster resources and ensure that many distributed services / users play nicely together. They're essential for any modern cluster deployment.
Generally these users are pretty happy; however we should reduce this barrier so that non-power-users with access to a cluster resource manager can use Dask on their cluster just as easily.
This raises the question of 'What should Dask provide and what should it leave to administrators?' It may be that the best we can do is to support copy-paste-edit workflows.
Because we've isolated all of the 'when' logic to the Adaptive code, the Marathon specific code is blissfully short and specific.
My hope is that people familiar with other cluster resource managers will be able to write similar objects and will publish them as third party libraries.

It's not clear that either solution is sufficient, or that the current implementation of either solution is any good. This is an important problem though as Dask.distributed is, today, still mostly used by super-users.
posts.key_insight: Complex deployment challenges are best addressed by separating policy decisions (when to scale) from implementation details (how to scale), enabling composable solutions that empower community contributions rather than requiring one monolithic answer.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin announces a new weekly development log series to increase transparency around Dask development, which has accelerated due to increased adoption and corporate funding. The post covers four main development areas: embedded Bokeh servers for worker diagnostics, a redesigned scheduler architecture that shifts intelligence to workers, new dataframe algorithms, and the fastparquet library. The scheduler changes move responsibility from a centralized scheduler to smarter workers, enabling better resource utilization and features like GPU constraints. These architectural changes are expected to yield around 30% speedup for dataframe operations while also simplifying the codebase for contributors.
key_points: Embedded Bokeh servers in workers now enable rapid diagnostic visualization development - creating a new diagnostic is faster than grepping through logs
The scheduler architecture has been inverted: workers are now smarter and receive all ready tasks immediately rather than being fed tasks just-in-time
Moving logic from scheduler to workers paradoxically made both components easier to reason about, lowering barriers for contributors
The changes enable resource constraints (GPU, RAM requirements) that the simpler workers couldn't handle
Fastparquet provides a Pythonic Parquet reader/writer using Numba with good performance
money_quotes: It is now faster for me to build a new diagnostic than to grep through logs.
Previously the scheduler knew everything and the workers were fairly simple-minded.
This is better locally but slightly worse globally.
By moving logic from the scheduler to the worker we've actually moved them both easier to reason about.
Increased activity is great, however an unintended side effect is that I have spent less time writing about development and engaging with the broader community.
Everyone running a Tornado application should have an embedded Bokeh server running.
posts.key_insight: Distributing intelligence from a centralized scheduler to smarter workers can improve both performance and code simplicity, even though it trades global optimization for local efficiency.

posts.summary: This development log documents a week of Dask improvements focused on expanding capabilities and optimizing performance at scale. The major feature addition allows dask.arrays to work without known chunk sizes, enabling conversion from dataframes to arrays for machine learning workflows. Performance work included reducing import times from over 1 second to 300ms and optimizing the scheduler for 1000+ worker clusters. The post also introduces experimental inter-client communication features and highlights the value of live monitoring dashboards for identifying performance bottlenecks in distributed systems.
key_points: Dask arrays can now operate without known chunk sizes, enabling dataframe-to-array conversion for ML workflows, though this blurs previously clear capability boundaries
Import times were reduced from over 1 second to 300ms through targeted optimization work
Live Bokeh dashboards revealed that worker-to-worker connection times of 10-100ms represent a significant performance bottleneck
New channel mechanism enables inter-client communication and collaborative workflows on shared clusters
The sortedcollections library provides pure-Python sorted containers at C-extension speeds, recommended for other library maintainers
money_quotes: We're increasing capability here but blurring the lines of what is possible and what is not possible, which used to be very clear.
It's stunning how clearly performance issues present themselves when you have the right monitoring system.
Already we can see that the time it takes to connect between workers is absurdly high in the 10ms to 100ms range, highlighting an important performance flaw.
It now takes literally one line of code to add a new plot to these pages.
This range of extension requests for the scheduler is somewhat large these days and we'd like to find ways to keep these expansions maintainable going forward.
posts.key_insight: Good monitoring systems make performance problems immediately visible, transforming debugging from guesswork into clear diagnosis.

posts.summary: This development log covers a week of work on Dask's distributed scheduler, focusing on rebuilding load balancing algorithms after a major rewrite of the worker and scheduler components. The author explains the complexity of task assignment across millions of tasks and thousands of workers, requiring near-optimal placement in near-constant time. He also discusses switching development clusters from Amazon EC2 to Google Container Engine, weighing the tradeoffs of each platform. The post concludes with a brief note on successful testing of fastparquet with S3 data.
key_points: Rewriting the scheduler and worker resulted in loss of load balancing algorithms, which had to be rebuilt from scratch while maintaining performance for power users
Dynamic load balancing must consider task runtime, data size and location, worker queue duration, and data locality—all computed in near-constant time for millions of tasks
The team's testing strategy evolved from fine-grained unit tests to behavioral integration tests and strict runtime validation due to repeated internal rewrites
Google Container Engine offers advantages like minute-based billing and native Kubernetes support, but Amazon has better Python tooling for S3 access
Fastparquet and Dask.dataframe worked out of the box with S3 data, though performance tuning is still needed
money_quotes: Careful dynamic load balancing is essential to running atypical workloads (which are surprisingly typical among Dask users)
Because the number of tasks can grow into the millions and the number of workers can grow into the thousands, Dask needs to figure out a near-optimal placement in near-constant time, which is hard.
Generally working on the Dask distributed scheduler has taught me the brittleness of unit tests.

As we have repeatedly rewritten internals while maintaining the same external API our testing strategy has evolved considerably away from fine-grained unit tests to a mixture of behavioral integration tests and a very strict runtime validation system.
We're doing what we can to create isolated tests for every failure mode that we find to make future rewrites retain good behavior.
I was surprised that everything seemed to work out of the box.
posts.key_insight: Building a distributed scheduler that handles millions of tasks requires abandoning traditional unit testing in favor of behavioral integration tests and runtime validation, because performance-based metrics are inherently difficult to capture in isolated tests.

posts.summary: This development log covers a week of work on Dask, focusing on performance improvements and benchmarking efforts. The team resolved load balancing issues that had degraded performance on messy workloads, returning bleeding-edge builds to full speed. A major breakthrough came from identifying that calculating the size of object dtype dataframes was causing 100ms+ gaps between worker tasks, which they fixed by using pessimistic estimates instead of accurate calculations. The author also began preliminary work on comparing Dask and Spark DataFrames, while thoughtfully considering how to conduct unbiased benchmarks in a competitive open source landscape.
key_points: Load balancing cleanup restored performance for bleeding-edge users after disruptive scheduler and worker changes
Worker lag of 100ms+ was traced to size calculations on object dtype dataframes, fixed by using pessimistic guesses instead of accurate results
Shuffle overhead was reduced by a factor of two through the lag fix, though 10ms inter-task lag remains on some workloads
The author is planning Spark/Dask DataFrame comparisons but wants to collaborate with Spark devs to avoid biased benchmarks
Community members are building out benchmarks using airspeed velocity at dask-benchmarks
money_quotes: Often this is quite fast, for example for numpy arrays we can just multiply the number of elements by the dtype itemsize. However for object dtype arrays or dataframes (which are commonly used for text) it can take a long while to calculate an accurate result here.
While 10ms may not sound like a long time, when we perform very many very short tasks this can quickly become a bottleneck.
However in today's climate where open source software developers have a vested interest, benchmarks often focus on a projects' strengths and hide their deficiencies.
Even with the best of intentions and practices, a developer is likely to correct for deficiencies on the fly. They're much more able to do this for their own project than for others'.
Benchmarks end up looking more like sales documents than trustworthy research.
My tentative plan is to reach out to a few Spark devs and see if we can collaborate on a problem set and hardware before running computations and comparing results.
posts.key_insight: Performance optimization often requires trading accuracy for speed, and honest benchmarking requires collaboration with competitors to avoid becoming sales documents.

posts.summary: This release announcement details Dask version 0.13.0, highlighting major improvements across arrays, dataframes, and distributed scheduling. Key additions include Python 3.6 support, Parquet file format integration, and significant algorithmic improvements to handle thousand-core deployments. The release introduces embedded Bokeh servers for visual diagnostics within the scheduler and workers, custom serialization for better inter-worker communication, and dataframe-to-array conversions to support machine learning workflows. The post also previews future development directions including communication optimization and third-party tool growth.
key_points: Thousand-core deployments have become common, requiring algorithmic changes from workstation-focused designs to true distributed algorithms with tree reductions and approximate methods
Parquet support via fastparquet brings 'Big Data' ecosystem compatibility to Python, with multiple competing implementations emerging
Scheduling logic has been pushed from scheduler to workers, enabling better hardware saturation and complex features like GPU resource constraints
Embedded Bokeh servers within scheduler and workers provide deep visual diagnostics with direct access to internal state
Custom serialization enables support for previously unserializable types like HDF5/NetCDF files and opens paths to GPU-GPU communication and cross-language worker support
money_quotes: Thousand-core Dask deployments have become significantly more common in the last few months.
Parquet has gone from inaccessible in Python to having multiple competing implementations, which is a wonderful and exciting change for the 'Big Data' Python ecosystem.
Now that workers are more fully saturated we've found that communication issues are arising more frequently as bottlenecks.
I hope that external projects like these become the main focus of Dask development going forward as Dask penetrates new domains.
This is also the first step towards two frequently requested features: Better support for GPU-GPU specific serialization options and passing data between workers of different runtime languages.
posts.key_insight: As Dask scales to thousand-core deployments, the project is transitioning from workstation-focused algorithms to true distributed computing patterns, while building the infrastructure for third-party tools to drive future growth.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how Dask DataFrames extend Pandas to handle big data across distributed clusters. Using the NYC Taxi dataset (60GB in memory), Rocklin walks through common operations including CSV reading, aggregations, groupbys, joins, shuffles, and time series analysis on an 8-node EC2 cluster with 64 cores. The post highlights that while distributed computing adds overhead compared to single-machine Pandas, Dask maintains familiar Pandas syntax while enabling analysis of datasets too large for a single computer. A key focus is the new Parquet support, which provides faster I/O and compatibility with Spark workflows.
key_points: Dask DataFrames coordinate hundreds of small Pandas dataframes across a cluster, using standard Pandas functions under the hood with minimal overhead

Shuffles (resorting data across nodes) are expensive in distributed systems - the set_index operation took 25-40 seconds for 60GB, demonstrating why distributed tools should only be used when single-machine solutions fail
Once data is properly indexed by datetime, time series operations and row lookups become extremely fast (40ms round trip - faster than movie frame rates)
Parquet format support enables faster I/O, columnar queries, and interoperability between Dask and Spark ecosystems
The familiar Pandas API means users can leverage existing knowledge including keyword arguments like those in pd.read_csv
money_quotes: Pandas provides an intuitive, powerful, and fast data analysis experience on tabular data. However, because Pandas uses only one thread of execution and requires all data to be in memory at once, it doesn't scale well to datasets much beyond the gigabyte scale.

This operation is expensive, far more expensive than it was with Pandas when all of the data was in the same memory space on the same computer. This is a good time to point out that you should only use distributed tools like Dask.dataframe and Spark after tools like Pandas break down.

We should only move to distributed systems when absolutely necessary. However, when it does become necessary, it's nice knowing that Dask.dataframe can faithfully execute Pandas operations, even if some of them take a bit longer.
For reference, 40ms is the delay between two frames in a movie running at 25 Hz. This means that it's fast enough that human users perceive this query to be entirely fluid.
These little functions represented by the rectangles in these plots are just pandas functions.
Dataframes are nice, certainly, but Dask's main strength is its flexibility to move beyond just plain dataframe computations to handle even more complex problems.
posts.key_insight: Dask DataFrames enable scaling Pandas workflows to distributed clusters by coordinating many small Pandas operations, but distributed computing should only be adopted when single-machine tools genuinely cannot handle the data size.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates using Dask arrays to process a large electron microscopy dataset (10,000 images) distributed across an Amazon EC2 cluster. The workflow covers loading images lazily via scikit-image, persisting them across 10 worker nodes, recentering images using Numba-accelerated centroid calculations, and performing FFT analysis after rechunking the data from time-slice to pixel-based partitioning. The post emphasizes how Dask provides NumPy-like abstractions over distributed data while honestly documenting failures like faulty load-balancing and communication bottlenecks encountered during the analysis.
key_points: Dask arrays can stitch thousands of lazy scikit-image calls into a single logical 3D array that distributes computation across a cluster
Numba's JIT compilation creates fast C-speed code that integrates seamlessly with Dask for distributed image processing
Rechunking from time-slice to pixel-based partitioning enables time-series operations like FFTs but requires expensive N-squared communication
The scheduler's load-balancing heuristics can fail when network bandwidth is unexpectedly slow, causing worse performance than letting busy workers finish
Real-world distributed computing involves communication bottlenecks, faulty heuristics, and I/O blocking issues that must be diagnosed and addressed
money_quotes: Electron microscopy may be generating the biggest ndarray datasets in the field - terabytes regularly.
We haven't done any actual work yet, we've just constructed a parallel array that knows how to get any particular slice of data by downloading the right image if necessary.

I looked up centroids in the Scikit-Image docs and came across a function that did way more than what I was looking for, so I just quickly coded up a solution in Pure Python and then JIT-ed it with Numba.

This shows how easy it is to create fast in-memory code with Numba and then scale it out with Dask.array. The two projects complement each other nicely, giving us near-optimal performance with intuitive code across a cluster.

Switching the chunk pattern back and forth like this is generally a very difficult operation for distributed arrays because every slice of the array contributes to every time-series. We have N-squared communication.
Inter-worker communication was around 100-300 MB/s (typical for Amazon's EC2) and CPU load remained high. We're using our hardware.
As always with all computationally focused blogposts we'll include a section on what went wrong and what we could have done better with more time.
posts.key_insight: Dask arrays provide a NumPy-like abstraction that lets you reason about distributed image data as a single logical array while handling the complexity of cluster communication, lazy evaluation, and parallel execution under the hood.

posts.summary: This development log covers two weeks of Dask project work, focusing on stability fixes for the distributed scheduler, NASA grant writing for climate science applications, and infrastructure improvements. A key bugfix in version 1.15.1 addressed task removal errors that only surfaced in serious production deployments. The post also highlights work on Dask-EC2 deployment tools, categorical data handling improvements, and a communication layer refactor to address inter-worker bottlenecks.
key_points: A subtle bug in the distributed scheduler showed how coupling mostly-working components can yield a faulty system, caught only in serious production deployments
The Dask-EC2 project oscillates between 'very useful' and 'horribly broken' states depending on maintenance attention
Categorical column handling is being improved to support unknown categories without requiring a full dataset read upfront
Inter-worker communication has become a dominant bottleneck after recent optimizations, prompting a major refactor
NASA grant work aims to enable 100TB-scale atmospheric and oceanographic science with Dask+XArray
money_quotes: This was a nice exercise in how coupling mostly-working components can easily yield a faulty system.

This project can be either very useful for people just getting started and for Dask developers when we run benchmarks, or it can be horribly broken if AWS or Dask interfaces change and we don't keep this project maintained.
Nothing here is ready for production. This blogpost is written in haste, so refined polish should not be expected.

The Dask scheduler was able to heal the missing pieces (using the same machinery that makes Dask resilience) and so we didn't notice the flaw until the system was deployed in some of the more serious Dask deployments in the wild.
This distinction may seem small, but it limits performance in a surprising number of real-world use cases.
posts.key_insight: Complex distributed systems can hide subtle bugs through their own resilience mechanisms, with flaws only emerging under serious production workloads.

posts.summary: This post positions Dask as a hybrid between big data frameworks (Hadoop/Spark) and task schedulers (Celery/Luigi/Airflow), arguing that this combination makes it uniquely suited for complex data science problems. Traditional big data systems excel at standard operations like map/reduce but struggle with messy, real-world data processing workflows that don't fit neatly into those paradigms. Task schedulers offer more flexibility but lack efficiency and inter-worker communication. Dask bridges this gap by supporting arbitrary task graphs while maintaining the scalability and resilience of distributed computing systems, demonstrated through a concrete data ingestion example using dask.delayed.
key_points: Big data frameworks like Hadoop/Spark work well for simple operations but become inflexible for complex, messy data science workflows
Task schedulers like Celery/Luigi/Airflow offer flexibility but sacrifice efficiency due to lack of inter-worker communication and optimized scheduling
Dask uniquely combines arbitrary task graph execution with distributed computing capabilities like resilience and efficient serialization
The dask.delayed decorator allows writing natural Python for-loop code that automatically builds computational graphs for distributed execution
This flexibility enables implementing sophisticated algorithms (time-series, linear algebra, ML) that would be difficult or impossible in traditional MapReduce frameworks
money_quotes: Most of the time this is great because most big data problems are pretty simple.
These tools generally fail when asked to express complex or messy problems.
This is, however, the core of what distinguishes Dask from other systems like Hadoop and Spark. Dask is incredibly flexible in the kinds of algorithms it can run.
At its core, it can run any graph of tasks and not just map, reduce, groupby, join, etc.

Dask's ability to write down arbitrary computational graphs Celery/Luigi/Airflow-style and yet run them with the scalability promises of Hadoop/Spark allows for a pleasant freedom to write comfortably and yet still compute scalably.
Enterprise data systems are sometimes messy.
posts.key_insight: Dask's power comes from combining the flexibility of arbitrary task scheduling with the scalability of big data frameworks, allowing complex real-world data problems to be expressed naturally in Python while still running efficiently at scale.

posts.summary: This development log covers two weeks of work on Dask's distributed scheduler, highlighting a micro-release with performance improvements for multi-dimensional array communication. A compression heuristic bug was discovered and fixed after bandwidth issues surfaced during image processing work. The post also covers the new ability to add futures to the as_completed iterator for asynchronous algorithms, experimental ZeroMQ support through a communication refactor, and collaboration with Capital One on parallel solvers for machine learning. The author emphasizes the importance of finding collaborators who are both performance-focused and committed to building good open source software.
key_points: A compression sampling bug was causing poor network performance for multi-dimensional arrays - Dask was taking much larger samples than intended when handling memoryviews
The as_completed iterator can now accept new futures while iterating, enabling more flexible asynchronous algorithm patterns
A communication refactor allows pluggable transport mechanisms including experimental ZeroMQ support, though stability issues remain
The new 'persist' verb has been mirrored to the single machine scheduler, enabling algorithms that work optimally across both schedulers
XArray's new release with better NetCDF support opens distributed array computing to atmospheric scientists, potentially Dask's largest scientific user community
money_quotes: We've gotten both incredibly positive and incredibly negative praise/warnings about using ZeroMQ.
It's not a great fit because Dask mostly just does point-to-point communication, so we don't benefit from all of ZeroMQ's patterns, which now become more of a hindrance than a benefit.
ZeroMQ support is strictly experimental and will likely stay that way for a long time. Readers should not get excited about this.
The possibility that we might tap into Infiniband someday and have the ability to manage data locally without copies (Tornado does not achieve this) is very attractive to some user communities.
This relatively simple change allows for the easy expression of a broad set of asynchronous algorithms.
Working with the developers at Capital One has been very rewarding.
posts.key_insight: Small API changes like allowing futures to be added to as_completed, combined with careful performance debugging of issues like compression sampling, compound to enable entirely new classes of distributed algorithms.

posts.summary: This post presents two straightforward methods for parallelizing Scikit-Learn operations using Dask: the Dask Joblib backend and the dklearn drop-in replacements for Pipeline and GridSearchCV. The Joblib approach allows existing sklearn code to scale across clusters by simply wrapping it in a context manager, while dklearn provides optimized replacements that handle nested parallelism better than the original sklearn implementations. Rocklin demonstrates a 4x speedup using dklearn's GridSearchCV compared to standard sklearn on the same task. He acknowledges these aren't perfect solutions—Joblib collects intermediate results unnecessarily and limits operations to parallel maps—but emphasizes they represent accessible 'low-hanging fruit' for users with available cluster resources.
key_points: Dask's Joblib backend enables cluster parallelization for any sklearn operation that accepts an n_jobs parameter with minimal code changes
Dask 'stole' the delayed decorator from Joblib, showing the cross-pollination between these parallel computing tools
dklearn's Pipeline and GridSearchCV replacements achieve ~4x speedup over sklearn (40s vs 10s) by better handling nested parallelism
Joblib's interface isn't ideal from Dask's perspective because it always collects intermediate results back to the main process rather than leaving them on the cluster
Running computation in a single process can be 30% faster than multiple processes due to communication overhead costs
money_quotes: However, neither of these techniques are perfect. These are the easiest things to try, but not always the best solutions. This blogpost focuses on low-hanging fruit.
Dask users will recognize the delayed function modifier. Dask stole the delayed decorator from Joblib.
If we can extend Joblib to clusters then we get some added parallelism from joblib-enabled Scikit-learn functions immediately.
Generally any operation that accepts an n_jobs= parameter is a possible choice.
It's actually about 30% faster to run this computation within the same single process.
These aren't particularly sophisticated, nor are they performance-optimal, but they are easy to understand and easy to try out.
It's not clear how Dask can help the sklearn codebase without being too invasive.
posts.key_insight: The easiest path to parallelizing sklearn workflows isn't always the most performant, but accessible drop-in solutions that provide immediate speedups are valuable even when they leave optimization on the table.

posts.summary: This post explores how Dask and TensorFlow can work together for distributed machine learning, demonstrating three integration patterns: simple data parallelism for hyperparameter searches, deployment of TensorFlow networks on existing Dask clusters, and data preprocessing pipelines. The author walks through a concrete example of distributed training using parameter servers, where Dask handles data preparation, TensorFlow network setup, and coordination while TensorFlow manages the actual training. The post emphasizes that both libraries are designed to play nicely together without requiring special integration efforts, thanks to their shared foundation in Python and the PyData ecosystem.
key_points: Dask can trivially set up TensorFlow networks, prepare/clean data, and coordinate training progress without special integration code
Distributed deep learning suffers from public excitement that may distort its utility - single-machine optimization and traditional ML techniques are often more effective
TensorFlow servers can run alongside Dask workers in the same processes, sharing memory spaces and enabling efficient data movement
The integration required only about 80 lines of code to launch TensorFlow servers alongside Dask workers
Both libraries work well together because they share Python and the PyData stack (NumPy/Pandas) as a common foundation
money_quotes: Distributed deep learning is fundamentally changing the way humanity solves some very hard computing problems like natural language translation, speech-to-text transcription, image recognition, etc. However, distributed deep learning also suffers from public excitement, which may distort our image of its utility.
Focusing on single machine computation is often a better use of time. Model design, GPU hardware, etc. can have a more dramatic impact than scaling out.
Traditional machine learning techniques like logistic regression, and gradient boosted trees can be more effective than deep learning if you have finite data.

There is no need for a separate collaborative effort to integrate Dask and TensorFlow at a core level. Instead, they are designed in such a way so as to foster this type of interaction without special attention or effort.
The fact that both libraries play nicely within Python and the greater PyData stack (NumPy/Pandas) makes it trivial to move data between them without costly or complex tricks.
This is also the first blogpost that I've written that, from a Dask perspective, uses some more complex features like long running tasks or publishing state between workers with channels.
posts.key_insight: Well-designed libraries that embrace Python's ecosystem can interoperate naturally without requiring explicit integration efforts, enabling sophisticated distributed workflows through composition rather than coupling.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin provides a development update on Dask covering work from early February 2017. The main focus is on profiling iterative machine learning algorithms through the Dask-GLM project, which revealed that scheduler overhead becomes a significant bottleneck when underlying NumPy operations are fast. This led to two graph optimization efforts: more aggressive task fusion for nearly-linear graphs and structural sharing to avoid copying task graphs. The post also covers experiments integrating Dask with TensorFlow, Keras, and XGBoost, plus ecosystem improvements including Google Cloud Storage support and tutorial updates.
key_points: Scheduler overhead becomes the dominant bottleneck when in-memory NumPy operations are very fast, as revealed by Dask-GLM benchmarks
New graph optimizations trade potential parallelism for reduced overhead by fusing 'diamond-shaped' nearly-linear task graphs
Structural sharing now prevents unnecessary graph copies, significantly reducing overhead for math-heavy array operations
TensorFlow and XGBoost integrations demonstrate how Dask can deploy and manage other distributed services
The true goal of technical blogposts is attracting potential developers and collaborators
money_quotes: This is mostly just because the actual in-memory NumPy operations are so fast; any sluggishness on Dask's part becomes very apparent.
There is a tradeoff here and we'd like to be able to exchange some parallelism (of which we have a lot) for less overhead.
The true goal of all blogposts is attracting the attention of other potential developers.
The challenge for me is that I have little concrete experience with the applications that these libraries were designed to solve.
Feedback and collaboration from open source developers who use these libraries in production is welcome.
Because of the experimental nature of this work we had labeled the library 'not ready for use', which drew some curious responses from potential users.
posts.key_insight: When low-level operations become fast enough, the coordination overhead of a distributed scheduler becomes the primary performance constraint, requiring optimization strategies that deliberately sacrifice some parallelism.

posts.summary: Dask version 0.14.0 introduces significant internal improvements focused on performance and flexibility. The release features structural sharing of graphs between collections, which dramatically reduces overhead when building repetitive computations—a critical improvement for petabyte-scale climate science analyses. The communications system has been refactored to be pluggable, enabling future support for alternative transport mechanisms like MPI for supercomputing environments. Additional improvements include expanded DataFrame API coverage, better handling of categorical data, and various distributed system enhancements for multi-client workflows.
key_points: Structural sharing of graphs reduces computation overhead by ~3.6x for repetitive operations, removing a blocker for petabyte-scale climate science analyses
The communications system is now pluggable, preparing Dask for exotic interconnects and potential MPI support in supercomputing environments
DataFrame improvements include fast categorical assignment without full data passes, groupby cumulative reductions, and Parquet append support
New DataFrame rendering shows metadata in a readable table format rather than cryptic internal representations
Distributed system gains scheduler-file option for easier deployment on shared network file systems common in academic institutions
money_quotes: Copying graph data structures with millions of nodes can take seconds and interrupt interactive workflows.
This was also one of the blockers identified by the climate science community stopping them from handling petabyte scale analyses.
This is especially important as dask gets used in institutions with faster and more exotic interconnects (supercomputers).
We've been asked a few times to support other transport mechanisms like MPI.
Because Dask.dataframes are lazy we don't have this data and so typically render some metadata about the dataframe.
posts.key_insight: Internal architectural improvements like structural sharing and pluggable communications may seem mundane, but they're often the critical enablers for scaling from toy problems to petabyte-scale scientific computing.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin candidly examines how he initially produced misleading benchmarks showing Dask was 5x faster than Spark, when the reality was much closer. He identifies five systematic biases that plague software benchmarks: judging competitors by your own objectives, lacking expertise with competing software, gravitating toward favorable cases, improving your own code during testing, and omitting negative results. After collaboration with Spark experts and honest self-reflection, he found both projects perform within a factor of two in most cases. The post serves as both a confession and a guide for readers to be more critical of benchmark claims.
key_points: Different projects optimize for different use cases—Pandas/Dask excel at CSV (finance origins) while Spark excels at JSON (web origins), making apples-to-apples comparisons inherently difficult
Using 'default settings' for fairness backfires because defaults encode assumptions about deployment environments that may favor one project
The emotional pleasure of seeing your project win creates unconscious bias toward exploring and emphasizing those winning scenarios
Optimizing your code during benchmarking while not giving competitors the same opportunity is a form of dishonesty
The only real solution to experience bias is collaborating with someone who primarily uses the competing system
money_quotes: These results were wrong. They weren't wrong in a factual sense, and the experiments that I ran were clear and reproducible, but there was so much bias in how I selected, set up, and ran those experiments that the end result was misleading.
It often takes months of hands-on experience to understand a project well enough to definitely say that you're not doing things in a dumb way.
It feels great to see your project doing well. This emotional pleasure response is powerful. It's only natural that we pursue that feeling more.
There is no motivation to publish negative results.
Mixed numbers can be informative. They build trust and community.
Technical honesty goes a surprisingly long way towards implying technical proficiency.
My standard cluster configurations were biased towards Dask before I even considered running a benchmark.
posts.key_insight: Even well-intentioned developers systematically fool themselves when benchmarking their own software against competitors, making honest self-assessment and external collaboration essential for credible comparisons.

posts.summary: This technical post demonstrates how to build distributed optimization algorithms using Dask, showcasing both simple array-based parallelism and more customized approaches using dask.delayed. The authors walk through implementing gradient descent and ADMM (Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers) for convex optimization problems, showing how researchers familiar with NumPy can easily transition to distributed computing. They benchmark dask-glm against scikit-learn on both a laptop and an EC2 cluster with 800 million rows of data. The key finding is that Dask provides a good balance between flexibility for interesting algorithms and ease of use for researchers without distributed systems backgrounds.
key_points: Dask enables researchers to write parallel algorithms using familiar NumPy syntax, requiring only minimal changes like using da.random instead of np.random and adding dask.compute calls
The dask.delayed API allows implementation of consensus-based distributed algorithms like ADMM through simple Python for loops and function calls
Dask-glm performs comparably to scikit-learn on single machines, but its real advantage is scaling to larger-than-memory datasets across clusters
Scheduler overhead becomes significant when individual tasks take only milliseconds, requiring careful chunk size selection
There's a gap in the community's understanding of optimization algorithms - many don't realize that different methods solve fundamentally different problems and aren't interchangeable
money_quotes: If you can write iterative array-based algorithms in NumPy, then you can write iterative parallel algorithms in Dask
Relatively few people understand both mathematical optimization theory and the details of distributed systems.

Creating distributed algorithms with Dask was surprisingly easy; there is still a small learning curve around when to call things like persist, compute, rebalance, and so on, but that can't be avoided.
If your problem fits in memory on a single machine you should continue to use Scikit-Learn and Statsmodels.

We've found that Dask provides a nice balance between being flexible enough to support interesting algorithms, while being managed enough to be usable by researchers without a strong background in distributed systems.
Working on algorithms in isolation is not nearly as fun as collaborating on them; please join the conversation and contribute!
There is a surprisingly large amount of misunderstanding in 'the community' surrounding what optimization algorithms do in the world of predictive modeling.
posts.key_insight: Dask bridges the gap between mathematical optimization researchers and distributed systems experts by allowing NumPy-like code to scale seamlessly from laptops to clusters.

posts.summary: This release announcement for Dask 0.14.1 details significant performance improvements and new features across arrays, dataframes, and the distributed scheduler. The array module now handles NumPy interoperability more smoothly with automatic chunking, and reshape operations support more complex transformations. DataFrame improvements include smarter set_index that automatically detects sorted columns and optimizes partitioning. The distributed scheduler gains powerful debugging capabilities that let users replay remote exceptions locally, plus new async/await syntax support, in-process transport for single-machine clusters, and connection pooling that reduces round-trip latency to 11ms.
key_points: Dask arrays now automatically chunk and convert NumPy arrays in mixed operations, improving usability
Slicing optimizations have been reworked to minimize inter-worker communication for distributed computing while still supporting the original HDF5/NetCDF single-machine use case
New recreate_error_locally feature brings remote exceptions back to local machine for debugging with standard Python tools like pdb
In-process transport allows single-machine users to access advanced distributed scheduler features like async computation and web diagnostics without network overhead
Set_index is now smarter, automatically detecting sorted columns and optimizing partition counts during shuffles
money_quotes: Debugging is hard in part because exceptions happen on remote machines where normal debugging tools like pdb can't reach.
Historically dask.arrays were used almost exclusively by researchers with large on-disk arrays stored as HDF5 or NetCDF files.
We have tons of distributed disk bandwidth but really want to avoid communicating large results between workers.
Supporting both use cases is possible and I think that we've achieved that in this release so far, but it's starting to require increasing levels of care.
We strongly recommend Parquet as the standard data storage format for Dask dataframes (and Pandas DataFrames).
It turns out that when you offer distributed grid search people quickly want to scale up their computations to hundreds of thousands of trials.
posts.key_insight: As Dask's user base expands from single-machine HDF5 researchers to distributed cluster computing, the optimization goals shift from disk bandwidth to minimizing inter-worker communication, requiring increasingly careful engineering to support both use cases.

posts.summary: This post demonstrates how to use Dask to distribute Pandas dataframes for data wrangling, then seamlessly hand them off to XGBoost for distributed gradient boosted tree training. The example uses airline delay prediction on a 10-node cluster, showing familiar Pandas-like operations at scale. The key insight is that dask-xgboost is only 200 lines of code because it simply co-locates XGBoost workers within Dask workers, allowing zero-cost data sharing between systems. Rocklin argues this 'serve other libraries' approach lets Dask leverage the existing open source ecosystem rather than reimplementing algorithms, enabling weekend-level integration efforts with fully-featured distributed ML solutions.
key_points: Dask-xgboost works by running XGBoost workers inside Dask worker processes, enabling zero-cost memory sharing between the two distributed systems
The entire dask-xgboost integration is only 200 lines of code and was built over a weekend, demonstrating the power of co-locating distributed systems
Dask's philosophy is to 'serve other libraries' rather than reimplement algorithms - Dask.arrays use NumPy, Dask.dataframes use Pandas, and distributed ML uses XGBoost
This same pattern was used for TensorFlow integration and could extend to MPI, Elemental, and other distributed systems
The familiar Pandas API means data scientists can work at cluster scale without learning entirely new tools
money_quotes: Dask has no need to make such an algorithm because XGBoost already exists, works well and provides Dask users with a fully featured and efficient solution.
Because both Dask and XGBoost can live in the same Python process they can share bytes between each other without cost, can monitor each other, etc.
These two distributed systems co-exist together in multiple processes in the same way that NumPy and Pandas operate together within a single process.
Sharing distributed processes with multiple systems can be really beneficial if you want to use multiple specialized services easily and avoid large monolithic frameworks.
We're only able to cover the breadth of functionality that we do today because we lean heavily on the existing open source ecosystem.
We get a fully featured solution that is maintained by other devoted developers, and the entire connection process was done over a weekend.
The point here isn't that we predicted airline delays well, it was that if you are a data scientist who knows Pandas and XGBoost, everything we did above seemed pretty familiar.
posts.key_insight: Co-locating multiple distributed systems in the same processes enables seamless data handoff and allows Dask to leverage specialized tools like XGBoost rather than building monolithic frameworks that reimplement everything.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin describes building an experimental Python streaming library as a learning exercise to understand systems like Storm, Flink, and Spark Streaming. The library provides a simple API for creating data pipelines with map/filter/reduce operations that push data through rather than pull with iterators. He demonstrates features like branching streams, back pressure handling via Tornado futures, and integration with Dask for distributed computing. While acknowledging the library lacks production features like fault tolerance and out-of-order element handling, Rocklin highlights its value as a lightweight, Pythonic alternative that's easy to understand and extend.
key_points: Traditional Python iterators become awkward for streaming when you need non-blocking operations, multiple inputs/outputs, or time-based windowing
The library uses a push-based model with emit() instead of pull-based next(), trading 10x performance overhead for flexibility
Back pressure is handled through Tornado futures, allowing downstream consumers to signal when they're ready for more data
The system can integrate with Dask for distributed computing, offloading the complexity of cluster management
The entire codebase is just a few hundred lines, making it maintainable and easy to understand compared to Java/Scala-designed streaming systems
money_quotes: This was originally an exercise to help me understand streaming systems like Storm, Flink, Spark-Streaming, and Beam, but the end result of this experiment is not entirely useless, so I thought I'd share it.

We've accomplished the same result as our infinite iterator, except that rather than pulling data with next we push data through with source.emit. And we've done all of this at only a 10x slowdown over normal Python iterators :)
What is presented here is not in any way a competitor to existing systems like Flink for production-level data engineering problems.
There is no magic in the common case. Everything up until time-handling runs with tools that you learn in an introductory programming class.

All other streaming systems were originally designed for Java/Scala engineers. While they have APIs that are clearly well thought through they are sometimes not ideal for Python users or common Python applications.
I genuinely don't know if this project is worth pursuing. This blogpost is a test to see if people have sufficient interest to use and contribute to such a library.
posts.key_insight: Building a minimal streaming library from scratch reveals that the core concepts are surprisingly simple, and a lightweight Pythonic implementation can provide real value even without production-grade features.

posts.summary: This technical post demonstrates how to implement asynchronous optimization algorithms using Dask's distributed computing framework. The authors compare synchronous (blocking) ADMM with two asynchronous variants that update parameters continuously as results arrive. They show that asynchronous approaches enable better cluster utilization and faster initial convergence by allowing more frequent parameter updates. The experiments reveal that while asynchronous methods incur slightly higher 'cost of agreement' due to workers using slightly stale parameters, they achieve convergence much faster than synchronous approaches. The post provides complete code examples and visual profiling comparisons on a toy dataset.
key_points: Asynchronous algorithms enable monitoring and adapting computations during execution, increasing information flow compared to lock-step batch-iterative approaches
Dask's concurrent.futures API with as_completed() allows building flexible asynchronous workflows where results are processed as they arrive
Batched asynchronous updates provide a natural defense against slow clients by processing all available results at once, smoothly transitioning back to synchronous behavior when overwhelmed
Asynchronous ADMM converges faster initially but incurs higher 'cost of agreement' because workers operate with slightly out-of-date parameter estimates
The client-side parameter update loop can become the bottleneck in large clusters, requiring batching strategies to maintain throughput
money_quotes: In a non-blocking or asynchronous algorithm we send out work and track results as they come in. We are still able to run commands locally while our computations run in the background.
This ability to monitor and adapt a computation during execution is one reason why people choose asynchronous algorithms.
Asynchronous algorithms enable increased flow of information around the cluster in comparison to more lock-step batch-iterative algorithms.
Rather than update our parameters one by one, we update them with however many results have come in so far. This provides a natural defense against a slow client.
The cost of agreement is higher for asynchronous algorithms, which makes sense because each worker is always working with a slightly out-of-date global parameter estimate, making consensus harder.
Blocked ADMM doesn't update at all until shortly after 5 seconds have passed, whereas async has already had time to converge.
posts.key_insight: Asynchronous distributed algorithms trade off perfect parameter consistency for dramatically faster convergence by enabling continuous information flow and adaptation during execution.

posts.summary: This development log covers a week of Dask-related project work, highlighting improvements to Joblib integration for accelerating Scikit-Learn code, new LogisticRegression estimators in dask-glm, Arrow-based Parquet support, and a new sparse array library. The post also discusses infrastructure improvements including better spill-to-disk behavior, AsyncIO client support, and TLS security features. A key theme emerges around making Dask work seamlessly with existing Python data science tools without requiring changes to those codebases. The post notably acknowledges that smarter algorithms often beat parallelism, as demonstrated when scikit-learn's improved algorithm outperformed dask-glm's parallel approach.
key_points: Dask can now serve as a Joblib backend with pre-scattering of data, allowing Scikit-Learn's RandomForest to scale on clusters without code changes
New dask-glm LogisticRegression estimator provides scikit-learn compatible interface, though benchmark comparison showed better algorithms beat parallelism
Dask.dataframe now supports both fastparquet and Arrow engines for Parquet files, with switching from CSV to Parquet often more effective than parallel computing
A new sparse array library provides n-dimensional sparse arrays that work seamlessly with dask.array, enabling distributed sparse computations
NumPy's new __array_ufunc__ protocol allows generic array code to work across NumPy, dask.array, xarray, and other array libraries
money_quotes: This just goes to show you that being smarter about your algorithms is almost always a better use of time than adopting parallelism.
As a gentle reminder, you can typically get much faster query times by switching from CSV to Parquet. This is often much more effective than parallel computing.
This is particularly nice because it allows Dask and Scikit-Learn to play well together without having to introduce Dask within the Scikit-Learn codebase at all.

When performing machine learning computations (which are fairly heavy-weight) disk access is now fast enough that I don't notice it in practice and running out of memory doesn't significantly impact performance.
This is a big step towards writing generic array code that works both on NumPy arrays as well as other array projects like dask.array, xarray, bcolz, sparse, etc.
posts.key_insight: The most effective performance improvements often come from smarter algorithms and better file formats rather than adding parallelism, though seamless integration between tools enables users to benefit from both approaches.

posts.summary: This release announcement for Dask 0.14.3 details a wide range of performance improvements and new features across the Dask ecosystem. Major additions include sparse array support, Arrow-based Parquet reading, customizable graph optimizations, and improved distributed computing capabilities. The release also introduces quality-of-life improvements like preload scripts for custom initialization, InfiniBand network interface support for HPC environments, and enhanced web diagnostics. The post reflects the collaborative nature of open source development, acknowledging contributions from numerous developers across multiple related libraries.
key_points: Dask arrays now support sparse arrays and mixed dense/sparse arrays through a new API-compatible sparse library
Parquet data can now be read using either fastparquet or Arrow/Parquet-CPP, giving users flexibility in their data access approach
Communities can now define their own optimization schemes, allowing different performance constraints to be addressed
The distributed scheduler's web diagnostics moved into the scheduler process, making new visuals easier to build but sharing CPU resources
New InfiniBand network interface support enables better performance on HPC supercomputers
money_quotes: As Dask has grown into more communities, each with strong and differing performance constraints, we've found that we needed to allow each community to define its own optimization schemes.

These features were heavily requested by the climate science community, which tends to serve both highly technical computer scientists, and less technical climate scientists who were running into issues with the nuances of chunking.
Hopefully this capability increases the use of both projects and results in greater feedback to those libraries so that they can continue to advance Python's access to the Parquet format.
This makes it easier for people to adapt Dask to their particular institution.
Early trials indicate that computations like scikit-learn's RandomForest scale nicely on a cluster without any additional code.
posts.key_insight: As Dask matures and expands into diverse communities with different performance requirements, the project increasingly focuses on extensibility and customization rather than one-size-fits-all solutions.

posts.summary: Dask 0.15.0 introduces several significant improvements including native NumPy ufunc support, a cleaner async client API, and better multi-client coordination primitives. The release also includes breaking changes: the dask.async module was renamed to dask.local due to Python 3.5 keyword conflicts, Bag.map no longer auto-expands tuples (enabling multi-bag mapping instead), and the experimental ZMQ backend was removed. Performance optimizations, dashboard styling improvements, and Joblib pre-scattering for scikit-learn workloads round out the release. The post acknowledges contributions from dozens of community members across both core repositories.
key_points: NumPy 1.13.0 ufuncs now work natively with Dask arrays, allowing seamless code that works with either array type
The async API was simplified: public methods like client.gather() now work in async contexts instead of requiring underscore-prefixed private methods
Breaking change: Bag.map removed auto-tuple expansion but gained the ability to map over multiple co-partitioned bags
New multi-client coordination primitives (Queues and Variables) enable better distributed workflows
Joblib/scikit-learn integration improved with pre-scattering to reduce repeated data transfer
money_quotes: Thanks to recent changes in NumPy 1.13.0, NumPy ufuncs now operate as Dask.array ufuncs.
This means that a non-trivial subset of the actual NumPy API works directly out-of-the box with dask.arrays.
This makes it easier to write code that seamlessly works with either array type.
While convenient, this behavior gave rise to corner cases and stopped us from being able to support multi-bag mapping functions.
This can significantly speed up some scikit-learn computations by reducing repeated data transfer.
As usual, a number of bugs were identified and resolved and a number of performance optimizations were implemented.
posts.key_insight: Strategic breaking changes that remove convenient but limiting behaviors often enable more powerful capabilities, as demonstrated by removing tuple auto-expansion to enable multi-bag mapping.

posts.summary: This technical tutorial demonstrates how to programmatically start Bokeh servers from within Python applications rather than from the command line. Rocklin argues that Bokeh's true value lies not in static visualizations but in serving live-streaming, interactive dashboards that update with real-time data. He provides progressively complex code examples, from a basic line plot to streaming data visualizations, culminating in a real-world example from Dask's diagnostic dashboard. The post is aimed at developers who want to embed Bokeh directly into their libraries rather than running it as a standalone application.
key_points: Bokeh's greatest strength is live-streaming interactive visualizations, not static web graphics
The ColumnDataSource keeps data synchronized between server and browser, handling all the websocket complexity automatically
Embedding Bokeh programmatically allows developers to integrate real-time diagnostics directly into their libraries
Bokeh works well with async applications like Tornado and Asyncio because it's built on Tornado
money_quotes: I find that Bokeh's true value is in serving live-streaming, interactive visualizations that update with real-time data.
I've found it incredibly useful and easy to deploy sophisticated and beautiful visualizations that help me understand the deep inner-workings of my system.
Doing this by hand generally means serializing your data on the server, figuring out how web sockets work, sending the data to the client/browser and then updating plots in the browser.
Bokeh handles this by keeping a synchronized table of data on the client and the server, the ColumnDataSource.
I've found the Bokeh server to be incredibly helpful in my work and also very approachable once you understand how to set one up.
posts.key_insight: Bokeh's ColumnDataSource abstraction eliminates the complexity of building real-time streaming visualizations by automatically synchronizing data between server and browser.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin advocates for using Apache Parquet instead of CSV for dataframe computations, demonstrating the dramatic performance difference with the NYC taxi dataset. Using CSV, a simple aggregation took nearly 4 minutes, while the same operation on Parquet completed in just 2.8 seconds. The post walks through practical examples using Dask.dataframe, showing how to read both formats and convert existing CSV data to Parquet. Rocklin acknowledges that while CSV is human-readable, Parquet's binary columnar format is far more efficient for computer processing, especially for large datasets that don't fit in memory.
key_points: CSV is convenient for humans to read but prohibitively slow for large-scale data processing, taking 4 minutes versus 3 seconds for the same computation on Parquet
Parquet's columnar storage allows reading only the columns you need without loading the entire dataset
Converting from CSV to Parquet is straightforward with Dask and yields immediate performance benefits even with default settings
Two Python libraries support Parquet: pyarrow (C++ bindings) and fastparquet (NumPy + Numba implementation), both capable for most use cases
Parquet enables efficient out-of-core computation, letting you work with datasets larger than available RAM without painful wait times
money_quotes: Same values, but now our computation happens in three seconds, rather than four minutes.
CSV is convenient, but slow
Parquet is faster
This lets us work from disk comfortably without worrying about how much memory we have.
So do yourself a favor and convert your data
We can read it directly... But computers are much more able to both read and navigate this data.
