posts.key_insight: Switching from CSV to Parquet can reduce data processing times from minutes to seconds, making it practical to work with large datasets directly from disk.

posts.summary: This post presents comprehensive benchmarks of Dask's distributed scheduler across various computational patterns, task durations, and cluster sizes up to 256 workers. The author tests embarrassingly parallel, tree reduction, nearest neighbor, and sequential workloads using tasks, arrays, and dataframes. Results show Dask handles 2000-3000 tasks per second with the scheduler, scales well to around 100 workers with naive settings, and can push tens of gigabytes per second. The post emphasizes that longer tasks scale better, and expert tuning (bigger workers, larger chunks) can significantly improve performance at scale.
key_points: Dask's scheduler maxes out at around 3000 tasks per second, meaning 100ms tasks can saturate about 300 cores
Dependencies (reductions, nearest-neighbor) add minimal overhead as long as parallelism remains abundant
Complex operations like array rechunking and dataframe shuffles are more costly but still scale decently to hundreds of workers
Expert tuning with bigger workers (16-core vs 2-core) and larger chunks (10,000x10,000 vs 2,000x2,000) significantly improves scaling
The benchmarks were run with naive, untuned settings - expert use could yield 5-10x improvement
money_quotes: This is a technical document, not a marketing piece.
All that being said the author of this blogpost is paid to write this software and so you probably shouldn't trust him.
The longer your individual tasks take, the better Dask (or any distributed system) will scale.

Adding dependencies is generally free in modest cases such as in a reduction or nearest-neighbor computation. It doesn't matter what structure your dependencies take, as long as parallelism is still abundant.
We estimate that expert use would result in about a 5-10x scaling improvement over what we'll see.
Still though, even with naive settings we're routinely pushing through 10s of gigabytes a second on a modest cluster.
Like all tools it has limits, but even under normal settings Dask should scale well out to a hundred workers or so.
posts.key_insight: Dask scales well to hundreds of cores with naive settings, but understanding task duration, chunk size, and worker configuration is key to pushing into the thousands-of-cores range.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin reports on collaborative work during the SciPy 2017 sprints exploring how to parallelize scikit-image using Dask. The post is essentially a brief announcement pointing readers to a Jupyter notebook containing the technical details of their investigation. This represents early exploration of integrating Dask's parallel computing capabilities with the popular image processing library.
key_points: Collaborated with scikit-image developers at SciPy 2017 sprints
Investigated parallelizing scikit-image operations using Dask
Work documented in a publicly available Jupyter notebook
money_quotes: This weekend at the SciPy 2017 sprints I worked alongside Scikit-image developers to investigate parallelizing scikit-image with Dask.
posts.key_insight: Dask can potentially be used to parallelize scikit-image operations, as explored during collaborative sprint work at SciPy 2017.

posts.summary: This is a release announcement for Dask version 0.15.2, focusing on stability improvements and bug fixes since the 0.15.0 release. The major changes include a reorganization of conda packages into dask-core, distributed, and dask metapackage to better serve both library developers and end users. The release also addresses deployment challenges in Docker environments with new address configuration options, fixes various resource leaks, and continues expanding the array and DataFrame APIs. The post also highlights cross-language development with Julia workers connecting to the Dask scheduler.
key_points: New conda package organization separates dask-core (minimal dependencies) from the full dask metapackage, allowing downstream libraries to depend only on what they need
Improved deployment support for Docker and complex networking with separate contact and listen address options for workers
Memory, file descriptor, and process leaks have been identified and fixed through community collaboration
Julia developers are building workers and clients that interoperate with the Dask distributed scheduler, demonstrating cross-language extensibility
John Kirkham has been promoted to core contributor with commit rights to the Dask organization
money_quotes: This organization is designed to both allow downstream libraries to only depend on the parts of Dask that they need while also making the default behavior for users all-inclusive.
This is especially helpful when the perspective of ones network can shift dramatically.
We're grateful to all those who were able to provide very detailed case studies that demonstrated these issues and even more grateful to those who participated in resolving them.
There is undoubtedly more work to do here and we look forward to future collaboration.
posts.key_insight: Good package design balances the needs of downstream library developers who want minimal dependencies with end users who want comprehensive functionality out of the box.

posts.summary: This post announces and documents the initial work on Pangeo, an NSF-funded collaboration between NCAR, Columbia University, and Anaconda to run Dask and XArray on HPC systems for climate data analysis. The team solved the HPC deployment problem by creating a dask-mpi executable that leverages MPI's universal support on supercomputers, even though Dask still uses TCP for actual communication. They developed workarounds for batch scheduler limitations by requesting many small jobs instead of few large ones, taking advantage of Dask's ability to dynamically add workers. The post also covers JupyterLab integration for remote interactive work and identifies network performance optimization as a key area for future improvement.
key_points: Dask deployment on HPC was solved by creating an MPI wrapper that translates 'How do I start Dask?' into 'How do I run this MPI program?' - a question HPC staff can answer
Interactive analysis conflicts with batch schedulers designed for non-interactive jobs, but requesting many small single-machine jobs starts faster than requesting one large allocation
Network bandwidth on Infiniband reaches ~1GB/s (10x faster than Amazon) but only 30% of the hardware's capability, likely due to Tornado's byte handling
XArray's early adoption of Dask means it predates distributed scheduler features like persisting data in RAM and parallel debugging
JupyterLab provides critical functionality for remote HPC work: file browsing, terminals, and visualization through a single SSH-tunneled connection
money_quotes: We've successfully translated the question 'How do I start Dask?' to the question 'How do I run this MPI program?' which is a question that the technical staff at supercomputer facilities are generally much better equipped to handle.
To be clear, Dask isn't using MPI for inter-process communication. It's still using TCP. We're just using MPI to launch a scheduler and several workers and hook them all together.
Many small jobs can start more quickly than a few large ones. These take advantage of holes in the schedule that are too small to be used by larger jobs.
Dask doesn't need to be started all at once. Workers can come and go.
For any commodity or cloud-based system this is very fast (about 10x faster than what I observe on Amazon). However for a super-computer this is only about 30% of what's possible.
If we can get near MPI bandwidth then that may help to reduce concerns for this performance-oriented community.
This grant isn't just about serving the scientific needs of researchers at Columbia and NCAR, but about building long-term systems that can benefit the entire atmospheric and oceanographic community.
posts.key_insight: Pragmatic integration with existing HPC infrastructure (MPI, batch schedulers) rather than fighting against it enables Dask to bring interactive, scalable data analysis to supercomputing environments.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin and Joris Van den Bossche dramatically accelerate Python's geospatial analysis stack by rewriting GeoPandas with Cython and adding Dask parallelization. The Cython rewrite provides 10-100x speedups by storing direct pointers to GEOS C objects instead of wrapping them in Python Shapely objects, achieving performance parity with PostGIS. Adding Dask parallelization provides an additional 3-4x speedup on multi-core machines. They demonstrate the improvement by reducing a 3-hour NYC taxi analysis to 8 minutes, though the work remains experimental and they actively seek contributors.
key_points: GeoPandas was slow because it wrapped each geometry in a Shapely Python object and iterated through them in Python, causing both iteration overhead and memory bloat
Cythonizing GeoPandas to directly loop over GEOS pointers achieved 10-100x speedups, matching PostGIS performance since both use the same underlying GEOS library
Dask-geopandas enables parallel processing across cores by partitioning data either naively (by row chunks) or spatially (by geographic region)
Data ingestion via Fiona/GDAL remains a bottleneck now that computation is fast - loading CSV files took half the total runtime in their demo
The work requires upstream Pandas changes to support custom block types for properly tracking GEOS geometry pointers
money_quotes: Python's Geospatial stack is slow. We accelerate the GeoPandas library with Cython and Dask. Cython provides 10-100x speedups.
So instead of using a Pandas object-dtype column that holds shapely objects we instead store a NumPy array of direct pointers to the GEOS objects.
Cythonized GeoPandas and PostGIS run at almost exactly the same speed. This is because they use the same underlying C library, GEOS.
Cities are now doing a wonderful job publishing data into the open. This provides transparency and an opportunity for civic involvement to help analyze, understand, and improve our communities.
The Python GIS stack now has a full-speed library that operates as fast as any other open GIS system is likely to manage.
I suspect that we can build an intuitive and performant parallel GIS analytics system with modest effort.
We believe that this project can have a large social impact and are enthusiastic about pursuing it in the future.
posts.key_insight: By eliminating Python object overhead through Cython and adding Dask parallelization, Python's geospatial stack can match the performance of established tools like PostGIS while maintaining Python's accessibility and ecosystem benefits.

posts.summary: This release announcement covers Dask version 0.15.3, focusing on stability improvements and bug fixes since the previous release. The major additions include masked array support in dask.array, improved memory management for workers with multi-tiered thresholds, and a new statistical profiling feature for performance diagnostics. The memory management system now uses actual system memory monitoring in addition to tracking individual data pieces, providing more robust handling of memory pressure. The post also documents a breaking change in how the --memory-limit parameter is interpreted.
key_points: Dask.array now supports masked arrays similar to NumPy, funded partly by the UK Met Office for the Iris project
Memory management has been overhauled to use system-level monitoring via psutil, with tiered responses at 60%, 70%, 80%, and 95% memory usage
A new statistical profiling feature polls worker threads every 10ms to track function usage, available through the diagnostic dashboard
The --memory-limit parameter meaning has changed from specifying the 60% threshold to specifying the full memory allocation
money_quotes: We dump data to disk if the process rises about 70% use, stop running new tasks if it rises above 80%, and restart the worker if it rises above 95%
Previously we only measured memory use by adding up the memory use of every piece of data produce by the worker. This could fail under a few situations
If you've never cared about this then you shouldn't start caring now
It provides information that is orthogonal, and generally more detailed than the typical task-stream plot
posts.key_insight: Robust distributed computing requires multiple layers of memory management that combine data-level tracking with system-level monitoring to handle both anticipated and unexpected memory pressure.

posts.summary: This post evaluates three Python libraries for Apache Kafka: kafka-python, PyKafka, and confluent-kafka. The author finds that confluent-kafka offers the best performance (50% higher consumption, 3x higher production throughput) but has minimal documentation and a Java-centric API. PyKafka, maintained by Parse.ly, provides a more Pythonic interface, better documentation, and a more accessible codebase for contributors. The author notes that raw performance may not matter for most applications, as Parse.ly itself doesn't even enable their C library optimizations in production. He concludes by expressing cultural preference for PyKafka while acknowledging Confluent's stronger long-term alignment with Kafka development.
key_points: Confluent-kafka is significantly faster (50% higher consumption, 3x production throughput) but has poor Python documentation and error messages
PyKafka offers a more Pythonic API, better documentation, and more accessible codebase written mostly in Python rather than C extensions
Performance differences may be irrelevant for most use cases - Parse.ly runs their production systems on the slow Pure Python implementation
PyKafka has more comprehensive testing including actual Kafka integration tests, while confluent-kafka only tests API without a real Kafka instance
The author is investigating async support for Dask streaming integration and found PyKafka maintainers more responsive to Tornado-related issues
money_quotes: It took me a few times to get confluent-kafka to work. It wasn't clear what information I needed to pass to the constructor to connect to Kafka and when I gave the wrong information I received no message that I had done anything incorrectly.

According to the PyKafka maintainers at Parse.ly they haven't actually turned on the librdkafka optimizations in their internal pipelines, and are instead using the slow Pure Python implementation, which is apparently more than fast enough for common use.
Getting messages out of Kafka just isn't their bottleneck.
I find that Python C extensions are not pleasant to work with, even if you are comfortable with C.
Culturally I am drawn to Parse.ly's PyKafka library. They're clearly Python developers writing for Python users.
However the costs of using a non-Pythonic system here just aren't that large (Kafka's API is small), and Confluent's interests are more aligned with investing in Kafka long term than are Parse.ly's.
posts.key_insight: When choosing between Python libraries, developer experience and API design often matter more than raw performance, since the fastest option may still be overkill for actual production needs.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin introduces a prototype project called streamz that handles continuous streaming tabular data using Pandas. The library addresses the challenge that traditional data analysis tools like NumPy and Pandas assume all data is known upfront, while real-world data from sources like financial feeds, IoT sensors, and web logs arrives continuously. Streamz provides a dataframe-like API that manages control flow, branching pipelines, and streaming algorithms while maintaining state for incremental computations. The post demonstrates integration with Jupyter notebooks for live-updating outputs and Bokeh for real-time plotting, while acknowledging significant gaps including parallel computing support, Kafka integration, and out-of-order data handling.
key_points: Traditional data analysis libraries assume complete data upfront, but streaming data requires maintaining state for incremental updates
Streamz provides a Pandas-like API for streaming data that handles control flow, branching, joining, and back pressure automatically
Simple streaming operations become complex with for-loops when dealing with multiple streams, rate limiting, or buffering
Live Jupyter integration enables responsive feedback with updating outputs as new data arrives
The project is experimental with known gaps in parallel computing, Kafka ingestion, out-of-order data handling, and performance optimization
money_quotes: Some data never stops. It arrives continuously in a constant, never-ending stream.
It's still possible to use NumPy and Pandas, but you need to combine them with some cleverness and keep enough intermediate data around to compute marginal updates when new data comes in.
We don't have a single mean result, we have continuous stream of mean results that are each valid for the data up to that point.

When our computations are linear and straightforward like this a for loop suffices. However when our computations have several streams branching out or converging, possibly with rate limiting or buffering between them, this for-loop approach can grow complex and difficult to manage.
We're relying heavily on the Pandas library which wasn't designed with rapidly changing data in mind.
But most importantly this needs use by people with real problems to help us understand what here is valuable and what is unpleasant.
posts.key_insight: Streaming data analysis requires rethinking traditional batch-oriented tools by maintaining incremental state and managing complex pipeline control flow, which streamz addresses by providing a familiar Pandas-like API over continuous data streams.

posts.summary: The post explores techniques for optimizing Python code that is bound by data structure access rather than numerical operations, using a benchmark representative of Dask's task scheduler. Working with Cython developer Stefan Behnel, Rocklin tests two optimization strategies: compiling with Cython (without type annotations) and interning strings for faster dictionary lookups. The combined approaches yield a 2-3x speedup on the toy benchmark, while PyPy performs even better at 4-5x faster. However, when applied to an actual Dask scheduler benchmark, none of these optimizations showed meaningful improvement, demonstrating the gap between toy benchmarks and real-world complexity.
key_points: Cython can provide modest speedups (30%) even for data-structure-bound Python code without type annotations, though far less than the 10-100x gains typical for numerical code
String interning improves dictionary lookups by allowing Python to use fast identity comparisons instead of full string equality checks
PyPy significantly outperforms Cython on data-structure-heavy workloads, achieving 4-5x speedups over baseline CPython
The Dask scheduler can run under PyPy while clients and workers use standard CPython for PyData compatibility
Toy benchmarks often fail to represent real application performance - the optimizations that worked on the simple benchmark showed no effect on actual Dask scheduler code
money_quotes: This is an atypical problem Python optimization because it is primarily bound by data structure access (dicts, lists, sets), rather than numerical operations commonly optimized by Cython.
Python is already decently fast here, typically within a factor of 2-5x of compiled languages like Java or C++, but still we'd like to improve this when possible.
This isn't huge relative to typical Cython speedups (which are often 10-100x) but would be a very welcome change for our scheduler, where we've been chasing 5% optimizations for a while now.
If you ensure that there is only one copy of every string then you only need to do identity comparisons like text is text.
So PyPy can be quite a bit faster than Cython on this sort of code (which is not a big surprise).
The Dask scheduler is significantly more complex and it is difficult to build a single toy example to represent its performance.
posts.key_insight: While Cython, string interning, and PyPy can significantly optimize data-structure-bound Python code in isolation, real-world applications like Dask's scheduler are too complex for simple benchmarks to predict actual performance gains.

posts.summary: This post announces Dask 0.16.0, a major release featuring the new Dask collection interface that allows any custom object to be treated as a first-class Dask collection by implementing specific protocols. The release includes significant infrastructure improvements including transactional work stealing to prevent duplicate task execution, task retry capabilities, and enhanced Parquet compatibility across Arrow and Fastparquet. Network bandwidth optimizations and Tornado 5 compatibility work are underway to support exotic hardware like Infiniband. The post also highlights the consolidation of machine learning functionality into dask-ml and improved coordination with XArray's 0.10.0 release.
key_points: The new Dask collection interface formalizes protocols like __dask_graph__() and __dask_keys__() that enable any custom object to integrate seamlessly with all Dask schedulers, already adopted by XArray
Transactional work stealing prevents the same task from accidentally running on multiple workers simultaneously, critical for tasks with side effects
Task retry functionality addresses one of the most requested features, particularly valuable for users treating Dask as a task queue rather than just a dataframe/array library
Parquet compatibility has been extended to support both PyArrow and Fastparquet for reading and writing, though complete cross-library compatibility remains challenging
Nested compute calls now work within tasks, allowing .compute() inside delayed functions to invoke the distributed scheduler
money_quotes: Any object that implements the methods described in that document will interact with all Dask scheduler features as a first-class Dask object.
Now all XArray classes (DataSet, DataArray, Variable) are fully understood by all Dask schedulers. They are as first-class as dask.arrays or dask.dataframes.

It is still possible for the same task to run twice, but now this only happens in more extreme situations, such as when a worker dies or a TCP connection is severed, neither of which are common on standard hardware.
The variety of parquet reader/writers and conventions out there makes completely solving this problem difficult. It's nice seeing the various projects slowly converge on common functionality.
Doing this carefully proved to require a surprising degree of detail.
posts.key_insight: By formalizing the Dask collection interface as a public protocol, any Python project can now create objects that integrate as first-class citizens with all Dask schedulers, enabling seamless distributed computing across the ecosystem.

posts.summary: This development log covers ongoing work in the Dask ecosystem, including a potential shift to more open community communication practices, a major scheduler rewrite to enable compiler optimizations at scale, new Kubernetes deployment tooling, and various bug fixes and feature additions. The post emphasizes transparency by sharing work-in-progress updates that aren't yet production-ready. A key theme is scaling Dask to handle 1000+ worker clusters, which has exposed scheduler bottlenecks requiring architectural changes. The work spans infrastructure, community governance, and keeping pace with the broader Python data ecosystem.
key_points: The distributed scheduler is being rewritten to work better with PyPy and Cython, moving from Python dictionaries to Python objects for potential C-speed optimization
Dask is migrating away from closed internal communication systems toward more open community channels
New Kubernetes and Helm Chart tooling makes deploying Dask clusters on cloud infrastructure significantly easier
The project must continuously update to maintain compatibility with evolving NumPy and Pandas releases
Adaptive deployment features that auto-scale clusters based on workload received significant bug fixes
money_quotes: To increase transparency I'm trying to blog more often about the current work going on around Dask and related projects.
When you start building clusters with 1000 workers the distributed scheduler can become a bottleneck on some workloads.
Compiler technologies like PyPy and Cython can optimize Python object access down to C speeds, so we're experimenting with switching away from Python data structures to Python objects.
We're now trying to migrate away from closed systems when possible.
This change will be invisible operationally (the full test suite remains virtually unchanged), but will be a significant change to the scheduler's internal state.
I am learning on the job with Helm here.
posts.key_insight: Scaling distributed systems to thousands of workers requires rethinking fundamental architectural decisions, and open source projects benefit from transparent communication about work-in-progress.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin announces Pangeo, a proof-of-concept deployment combining JupyterHub, Dask, and XArray on Google Container Engine to enable atmospheric and oceanographic scientists to analyze large datasets in the cloud. The system allows users to log in, launch Jupyter notebooks, spin up Dask clusters, and process data stored in cloud storage without managing their own infrastructure. The project was built in just a few weeks through collaboration across multiple institutions, mixing academics, staff, and professional software developers. While still immature and experimental, the deployment has revealed important lessons about cloud file formats, environment customization, and the need for cross-community collaboration.
key_points: The Pangeo system integrates JupyterHub, Dask, XArray, Kubernetes, and cloud storage to democratize access to distributed computing for scientists
Traditional HDF5/NetCDF file formats perform poorly on cloud storage due to many small reads; alternatives like Zarr show promise but lack established trust
The rapid development was possible because of cross-institutional collaboration mixing scientists, academic staff, and professional developers
Environment customization remains challenging when distributed workers need the same packages as notebook sessions
The cloud-agnostic approach using Kubernetes allows deployment across Google, AWS, and Azure
money_quotes: Libraries like Dask and XArray already solve this problem computationally if scientists have their own clusters, but we seek to expand access by deploying on cloud-based systems.

Notice the mix of academic and for-profit institutions. Also notice the mix of scientists, staff, and professional software developers. We believe that this mixture helps ensure the efficient construction of useful solutions.
The file formats used for this sort of data are pervasive, but not particularly convenient or efficient on cloud storage.
When we try new problems in new environments we often uncover new opportunities to improve Dask's internal scheduling algorithms.
We found that this project aligns well with current efforts from many government agencies to publish large datasets on cloud stores.
However it is also open, and instructions to deploy your own live here.
posts.key_insight: Democratizing access to distributed computing for scientists requires not just technical integration of tools like Dask and Kubernetes, but also cross-institutional collaboration between academics, engineers, and open source communities.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin argues that programmers should prioritize writing simple, maintainable code over demonstrating technical sophistication. He observes that while education rewards increasingly complex solutions, professional programming requires the opposite mindset—solving problems with minimal code that others can easily understand and extend. Software should be viewed as a cost rather than an achievement, and every line written imposes a burden on reviewers and future maintainers. The best contributions to open source projects often involve removing code rather than adding it, and true expertise is demonstrated by solving hard problems with basic techniques.
key_points: Software is a cost, not an asset—every line written takes time from reviewers, maintainers, and future developers who could be spending that time elsewhere
The academic progression from simple to sophisticated techniques creates habits that are counterproductive in professional collaborative development
Simple code enables junior programmers to contribute and extend solutions, multiplying the impact of senior developers
Advanced techniques should only be used when simpler approaches genuinely cannot solve the problem
Demonstrating value as a programmer means solving hard problems simply, not showing off clever tricks
money_quotes: The best way you can contribute to an open source project is to remove lines of code from it.
Software is the currency that we pay to solve problems, which is our actual goal. We should endeavor to build as little software as possible to solve our problems.
Every line that you write costs people time.
So when you add code to a project you should feel meek. It should feel as though you are eating with your family and there isn't enough food on the table.
Solving problems with less code is hard, but it is a burden that you take on yourself to lighten the burdens of others.
We should use advanced techniques only when we are not smart enough to figure out how to use more common techniques.
Simple code enables others and boosts our impact. We demonstrate our value by solving hard problems with only basic techniques.
If you are a good programmer then you don't need to demonstrate that you know cool tricks.
posts.key_insight: True programming expertise is demonstrated not by using sophisticated techniques, but by solving hard problems with simple code that enables others to contribute.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin argues that the Python numeric computing community should adopt Numba more widely in open source libraries. Numba consistently outperforms or matches Cython in benchmarks while being dramatically simpler to write—just adding a decorator to plain Python code. The historical barriers to adoption were installation difficulties (LLVM dependency), community trust issues (corporate development at Anaconda), and lack of interpretability compared to Cython's C output. Rocklin contends that recent changes have addressed the installation problem (pip-installable llvmlite) and community concerns (Moore Foundation funding, open development), making Numba ready for broader adoption. However, he acknowledges legitimate remaining concerns: Numba's relative youth, difficulty debugging when things go wrong, and the black-box nature of its LLVM output.
key_points: Numba consistently matches or beats Cython performance while requiring far less code complexity—often just a decorator on plain Python
Historical adoption barriers included LLVM installation difficulties, distrust of Anaconda's corporate control, and the black-box nature of compiled output
The pip-installable llvmlite package and Moore Foundation funding have addressed installation and governance concerns
Numba's debugging experience remains problematic—when it doesn't work, diagnosing issues is extremely difficult compared to Cython's annotated HTML output
Community experts suggest Numba may struggle with larger codebases (>1000 LOC) where its limitations become more evident
money_quotes: Technical excellence alone is insufficient, and must be considered alongside community and long-term maintenance concerns.

I have years of experience with Cython, and in this function I've used every Cython optimization there is… By comparison, the Numba version is a simple, unadorned wrapper around plainly-written Python code.
A speedup by a factor of about 400, just by applying a decorator to the function.
Numba allows for speedups comparable to most compiled languages with almost no effort.
If your concern was about Numba trapping users into a for-profit mode, then that seems to have been resolved years ago.

Subjectively, Numba feels hard to debug, has cryptic error messages, and seemingly inconsistent behavior. It is not a 'decorate and forget' solution; instead it always involves plenty of fiddling to get right.

I think it took PyPy at least 5-7 years to reach a point where it was stable enough to really trust. Cython is 10 years old. People have good reason to be conservative with taking on new core dependencies.
posts.tone: advocacy with acknowledged caveats—optimistic about Numba's potential while honestly presenting counterarguments and expert skepticism
posts.key_insight: Numba's technical superiority in performance and usability is now matched by improved installation and governance, but the Python community's conservative approach to dependencies remains a legitimate barrier given debugging difficulties and the project's relative youth.

posts.summary: The post examines the challenge of accessing multi-dimensional scientific data (stored in HDF/NetCDF formats) from cloud storage systems like S3 and GCS. While organizations like NASA are uploading petabytes of HDF data to the cloud, the format's complex metadata structure makes efficient remote access nearly impossible—users must download entire files to extract small portions. Rocklin evaluates five potential solutions: using existing cloud-optimized formats, FUSE filesystem mounting, custom HDF readers, distributed services, and new purpose-built formats like Zarr. He concludes that the community faces a critical decision between fixing HDF's cloud limitations or adopting newer formats, with urgency mounting as NASA's data volumes are projected to grow tenfold by 2025.
key_points: HDF files scatter metadata throughout the file, requiring dozens of small reads that become prohibitively slow over network connections to cloud storage
Organizations like NASA are uploading massive HDF datasets to cloud storage that effectively cannot be efficiently accessed, missing the potential of cloud-hosted public data
Existing cloud-optimized formats (GeoTIFF, Parquet) work well but lack the multi-dimensional blocked array capabilities that scientific computing requires
New formats like Zarr and N5 have independently converged on similar specifications—storing array blocks as separate objects with centralized metadata—suggesting a sensible common solution
The scientific community must weigh the difficulty of retrofitting HDF for cloud efficiency against the institutional inertia of shifting to new standards
money_quotes: So organizations like NASA are dumping large amounts of HDF onto Amazon's S3 that no one can actually read, except by downloading the entire file to their local hard drive, and then pulling out the particular bits that they need with the HDF library.
This is inefficient. It misses out on the potential that cloud-hosted public data can offer to our society.
The HDF format is complex and metadata is strewn throughout the file, so that a complex sequence of reads is required to reach a specific chunk of data.
This unintended uniformity is a good sign. It means that both developer groups were avoiding creativity, and have converged on a sensible common solution.
However, technical merits are not alone sufficient to justify a shift in data format, especially for archival datasets of record that we're discussing.
The institutions in charge of this data have multi-decade horizons and so move slowly.
From its current cumulative archive size of almost 22 petabytes (PB), the volume of data in the EOSDIS archive is expected to grow to almost 247 PB by 2025.
posts.key_insight: The scientific community faces an urgent infrastructure decision: either retrofit the legacy HDF format for cloud storage efficiency or embrace newer formats like Zarr that were designed for distributed access from the start.

posts.summary: This guest post by Rich Postelnik demonstrates how a large retail bank uses Dask to manage complex credit modeling computations. The problem involves hundreds of interdependent equations for estimating loan default likelihood, which creates a challenging dependency and ordering problem. By using dask.delayed decorators, they transform Python functions into graph vertices that automatically track dependencies, eliminating manual argument passing and messy code. The approach allows analysts to write isolated equation functions while Dask handles graph generation, computation ordering, and optional distribution across clusters. The resulting system separates model code from computational infrastructure, enabling easier maintenance and scaling to larger-than-memory datasets without rewriting code for tools like Spark.
key_points: Credit modeling involves hundreds of interdependent equations with complex input/output relationships, making manual dependency tracking error-prone and code maintenance difficult
Dask's delayed decorator transforms regular Python functions into DAG vertices, automatically constructing computation graphs when delayed outputs are passed as inputs to other delayed functions
The bank created a custom Equation data structure that marks inputs and outputs, enabling automatic topological sorting and graph generation
Tree reduction patterns enable scalable aggregation of results across many applicants while creating visually structured computation graphs
Dask provides additional benefits beyond task scheduling: built-in diagnostics, seamless distribution to clusters, and DataFrame APIs accessible to less technical analysts
money_quotes: This boils down to a dependency and ordering problem known as task scheduling.
We want to avoid manually keeping track of the dependencies, as well as messy code.
It is this passing of Delayed objects as inputs to other delayed functions that allows Dask to construct the task graph.
This allows us to write each equation as its own isolated function and mark its inputs and outputs.
With our Dask-based data structure, we spend more of our time writing model code rather than maintenance of the engine itself.
This allows a clean separation between our analysts that design and write our models, and our computational system that runs them.
Now if I want to run our model across larger-than-memory data or on a distributed cluster, we don't have to worry about rewriting our code to incorporate something like Spark.
posts.key_insight: Complex computational dependency problems like credit modeling can be elegantly solved by using Dask's delayed decorator to automatically construct and execute task graphs, separating business logic from computational infrastructure.

posts.summary: Dask 0.17.0 is a significant major release featuring new task ordering heuristics for better memory management, improved Scikit-Learn integration through Joblib for distributed machine learning, and a major internal refactor of the scheduler from dictionaries to objects. The release also introduces user-settable task priorities and deprecates older rolling methods and the dask-ec2 project in favor of Kubernetes deployments. While the internal refactor didn't achieve its original performance goals with Cython or PyPy, it improved code readability for new developers. The release acknowledges contributions from over 40 developers across the dask and distributed repositories.
key_points: New task prioritization heuristics improve memory efficiency when traversing complex computations
Enhanced Joblib integration allows Dask to distribute Scikit-Learn algorithms across clusters and handle nested parallel computations without oversubscribing hardware
Major scheduler refactor from dictionaries to objects improved code readability but didn't achieve hoped-for Cython/PyPy performance gains
User-settable priorities now allow fine-grained control over task execution order
The project is moving away from dask-ec2 toward Kubernetes for cluster deployment across cloud providers
money_quotes: As Dask encounters more complex problems from more domains we continually run into problems where its current heuristics do not perform optimally.
The proliferation of state dictionaries was not highly discoverable.
Goal one ended up not working out. We have not yet been able to make the scheduler significantly faster under Cython or PyPy with this new layout.
However we have been happy with the results in code readability, and we hope that others find this useful as well.
We generally recommend kubernetes instead both for Amazon's EC2, and for Google and Azure as well.
posts.key_insight: Open source projects must balance ambitious technical goals like performance optimization against pragmatic improvements like code readability, and sometimes a refactor's secondary benefits outweigh its original objectives.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin argues that creating minimal, reproducible bug reports is essential for getting issues resolved quickly in open source projects. He explains that maintainers have limited time and bandwidth, so bug reports that are easy to understand and reproduce get prioritized over vague or complex ones. The post provides practical guidance on stripping away unnecessary context to isolate the core problem. Rocklin emphasizes that this skill benefits both maintainers and reporters, as it often leads to self-discovery of the actual issue.
key_points: Minimal bug reports dramatically increase the likelihood of getting help from maintainers
The process of minimizing a bug report often reveals the root cause to the reporter themselves
Maintainers triage based on how easy issues are to understand and reproduce
Good bug reports remove all unnecessary dependencies, data, and context
Learning to create minimal reproductions is a valuable software engineering skill
money_quotes: The best way to get help with a bug is to make it trivially easy for a maintainer to understand and reproduce.
Maintainers are busy. They triage issues by how easy they are to engage with.
Half the time, the process of minimizing your bug report reveals the cause of the bug.
Strip away everything that isn't essential to triggering the problem.
A good bug report is a gift to the maintainer.
posts.key_insight: Creating minimal bug reports is a craft that benefits everyone: maintainers get actionable issues, and reporters often solve their own problems in the process of simplification.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin outlines several potential summer projects for students interested in contributing to the Scientific Python ecosystem, particularly around Dask. He establishes clear criteria for good projects: well-defined scope, achievable in months, useful but peripheral enough that core devs won't complete them first, interesting, skill-building, and leading to future work. The proposed projects span distributed GPU arrays, numerical simulations with Numba+Dask, blocked linear algebra, porting Dask to R/Julia, and symbolic NumPy optimizations. Rocklin explicitly notes he offers ideas and context but neither funding nor mentorship.
key_points: Good summer projects should be well-scoped, completable in months, useful but peripheral, and teach valuable skills for future employment
Dask arrays can work with any NumPy-like interface, opening possibilities for distributed GPU computing via CuPy or Bohrium
Python with Numba and Dask may be competitive with C++/Fortran+MPI for numerical simulations if the performance gap is only ~10%
Dask's distributed scheduler is intentionally language-agnostic, making ports to R or Julia feasible by rewriting only client and worker code
A symbolic NumPy representation could enable optimizations, remote computation, visualization, and better memory/compute warnings
money_quotes: To be perfectly clear I'm only providing ideas and context here, I offer neither funding nor mentorship.

It's useful, but also peripheral. It has value to the ecosystem but is not critical enough that a core dev is likely to complete the task in the next few months, or be overly picky about the implementation.

Certainly fine-tuned C++/Fortran + MPI can out-perform Numba and Dask, but by how much? If the answer is only 10% or so then it could be that the lower barrier to entry of Numba, or the dynamic scaling of Dask, can make them competitive in fields where Python has not previously had a major impact.
For which kinds of problems is a dynamic JITted language almost-as-good as C++/MPI?
Presumably R and Julia user communities don't want exactly Pandas-style dataframe semantics anyway.
Projects like Numpy and Dask array compute what the user says, even if a more efficient solution exists.
posts.key_insight: The best open source summer projects are peripheral enough that maintainers won't be territorial, yet valuable enough to teach real skills and make meaningful ecosystem contributions.

posts.summary: This release announcement details Dask version 0.17.2, highlighting several technical improvements including Tornado 5.0 integration enabling 3GB/s Infiniband bandwidth, a new HTTP file system for reading data from web servers, and a critical correctness bug fix in dataframe shuffles. The release also introduces a unified Cluster superclass that standardizes deployment interfaces across different resource managers, along with improved adaptive deployment logic that automatically scales clusters based on workload. The post acknowledges the collaborative nature of open source development by listing dozens of contributors across both the main dask and distributed repositories.
key_points: Tornado 5.0's new IOStream.read_into method enables Dask to achieve full 3GB/s Infiniband bandwidth on high-performance computing systems with fewer memory copies
New HTTP/HTTPS file system support allows reading data directly from web servers with range query support for random access
A correctness bug in dataframe shuffle operations that dropped rows during joins and groupby-applies with many partitions was fixed
A unified Cluster superclass now provides consistent interfaces across Yarn, SGE, Kubernetes, PBS and other deployment systems
Adaptive deployments now include workload-based worker estimation, min/max limits, and hysteresis prevention
money_quotes: On the Cheyenne system, where we tested this, we were able to get the full 3GB/s bandwidth available through the Infiniband network with this change
Adaptive deployments allow clusters to scale themselves automatically based on current workload
If you have recently submitted a lot of work the scheduler will estimate its duration and ask for an appropriate number of workers to finish the computation quickly
Now that we have a consistent interface we have started to invest more in improving the interface and intelligence of these systems as a group
We identified and resolved a correctness bug in dask.dataframe's shuffle that resulted in some rows being dropped during complex operations
posts.key_insight: Standardizing deployment interfaces across different cluster managers enables the Dask project to invest in shared improvements like intelligent adaptive scaling that benefit all deployment environments simultaneously.

posts.summary: The post argues that Python's array computing ecosystem has organically grown to support GPUs, sparse arrays, and distributed computing through libraries like CuPy, Sparse, and Dask that implement the Numpy API. However, this decentralized growth has created a costly pair-wise integration problem where each library must build custom plugin systems to work with others. Rocklin proposes two solutions: either create a centralized 'arrayish' dispatch library or build dispatch protocols directly into Numpy itself. He favors the Numpy approach, citing the __array_ufunc__ protocol as precedent, though acknowledges Numpy's historically slow pace of change. The post concludes that transitioning from pair-wise to ecosystem-wide protocols is necessary and achievable, as the community has successfully done before with Numeric/Numarray consolidation and scikit-learn's fit/predict API.
key_points: Libraries like CuPy, Sparse, and Dask implement the Numpy API for different hardware (GPUs, sparse data, distributed clusters), keeping the API alive even as implementations evolve
Current pair-wise plugin systems where each library must know about every other library create an expensive n×m maintenance burden that doesn't scale
Two solutions exist: a new centralized dispatch library ('arrayish') or building dispatch mechanisms into Numpy itself via protocols like __array_ufunc__
The Numpy protocol approach is preferable because everyone already aligns to Numpy's decisions and less code rewriting is needed
Python's array ecosystem needs to transition from decentralized organic growth to coordinated community-standard protocols
money_quotes: The Numpy API lives on in successor projects.
Building, maintaining, and extending these plugin mechanisms is costly.
Pair-wise plugin mechanisms make sense for a single project, but are not an efficient choice for the full ecosystem.
This reduces the big n by m cost of maintaining several plugin systems, to a more manageable n plus m cost of using a single plugin system in each library.

To solidify this growth and apply it across the ecosystem we now need to do some central planning to move from a pair-wise model where packages need to know about each other to an ecosystem model where packages can negotiate by developing and adhering to community-standard protocols.
The community has done this transition before (Numeric + Numarray -> Numpy, the Scikit-Learn fit/predict API, etc..) usually with surprisingly positive results.
I recommend that all Numpy-API compatible array projects implement the __array_ufunc__ protocol.
posts.key_insight: Python's array computing ecosystem has outgrown pair-wise library integrations and needs community-standard protocols—preferably built into Numpy—to enable seamless interoperability between GPU, sparse, and distributed array implementations.

posts.summary: This release announcement details Dask version 0.18.0, a major release that begins the housecleaning process in preparation for a 1.0 release. The update introduces breaking changes including centralized configuration management, replacing the 'get=' keyword with 'scheduler=', and consolidating runtime options under dask.config.set. New features include transparent support for NumPy Generalized Universal Functions, automatic chunk sizing, new file format support (ORC, JSON, Zarr), and improvements to array algorithms like einsum and QR decomposition. The release emphasizes cleaner interfaces while acknowledging that heavy users may experience mild friction during the transition.
key_points: Dask is preparing for a 1.0 release by cleaning up interfaces and consolidating configuration management across all subprojects
The new centralized configuration system unifies settings from config files, environment variables, and runtime code into dask.config module
Breaking changes replace generic 'get=' keyword with clearer 'scheduler=' keyword for selecting execution backends
New 'auto' chunking helps users achieve optimal chunk sizes without manual calculation
GUFunc support enables seamless interoperability with NumPy and Numba without any coordination between projects
money_quotes: The Dask core library is nearing a 1.0 release. Before that happens, we need to do some housecleaning.
As happens with any release that starts breaking things, many other smaller breaks get added on as well.
I'm personally very happy with this release because many aspects of using Dask now feel a lot cleaner, however heavy users of Dask will likely experience mild friction.

What I like about this is that Dask and Numba developers didn't coordinate at all on this feature, it's just that they both support the Numpy GUFunc protocol, so you get interactions like this for free.

To be clear, this doesn't support 'automatic chunking', which is a very hard problem in general. Users still need to be aware of their computations and how they want to chunk, this just makes it marginally easier to make good decisions.
We've found that different computing cultures like to specify configuration in several different ways.
posts.key_insight: Major version releases provide an opportunity to clean up accumulated technical debt and unify disparate subsystem interfaces, even if it causes short-term friction for existing users.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin addresses misconceptions about Dask's scaling capabilities, clarifying that while Dask began focused on single-node parallelism for medium-sized datasets, it has grown to handle distributed computing across thousands of nodes. The post demonstrates a 50-node cluster handling a terabyte dataset as a 'modest' example, noting this could scale two more orders of magnitude. Rocklin then catalogs the practical challenges users face when scaling out, from file handle limits and scheduler overhead to graph serialization times and dashboard usability. The core tension he identifies is that Dask's flexibility and power make it harder to protect users from mistakes at scale—constrained systems are easier to make foolproof. He concludes that standard workflows like Dask dataframe will generally work fine, but custom algorithms at thousand-node scale require genuine expertise.
key_points: Dask maintains two separate schedulers—one optimized for single-node simplicity, another for distributed scaling—allowing the same API to work from laptops to thousand-node clusters
The scheduler has roughly 200 microseconds overhead per task, creating an inverse relationship between task duration and maximum core saturation
Task graphs can consume around 1kB of memory per task, meaning million-node graphs require careful management and gigabytes of scheduler memory
The combination of scalability and user freedom makes it impossible for Dask to fully protect users from breaking things—constrained systems are easier to make foolproof
At scale, users often accidentally overwhelm external components like distributed storage or databases that were designed for single-machine access patterns
money_quotes: High performance computing isn't about doing any one thing well, it's about doing nothing badly.
Dask provides users more power than they're accustomed to. It's easy for them to accidentally clobber some other component of their systems.
It's much easier to protect users when you can constrain what they can do.

Many of these systems provide abstractions that are very well tested and stable for normal single-machine use, but that quickly become brittle when you have a thousand machines acting on them with the full creativity of a novice user.
We still believe in the efficiency of single-node parallelism.
If you press compute and nothing shows up on the dashboard for a minute or two, this is what's happening.
Seeing the start and stop time of every task of a million-task computation just isn't something that our brains can fully understand.
posts.key_insight: Dask's power comes from giving users freedom to build custom distributed systems, but that same freedom means scaling to thousands of nodes requires genuine expertise—the framework can't fully protect you from yourself.

posts.summary: This development log updates the Dask community on four active workstreams in June 2018: Yarn deployment for Hadoop clusters, expanded machine learning examples, incremental training capabilities, and HPC deployment improvements. The Yarn integration, built on Jim Crist's skein project, finally answers the common question of how to deploy Dask on enterprise Hadoop/Spark clusters. The incremental training wrapper enables Scikit-Learn estimators to train on large datasets in batches, trading parallelism for pragmatism. The post also announces a new initiative to collect user stories, acknowledging that Dask needs help with community building as much as technical development.
key_points: Yarn deployment is now possible through dask-yarn and skein, making Dask a first-class citizen on Hadoop/Spark/Hive clusters that previously required Java APIs
Incremental training wraps Scikit-Learn estimators with partial_fit support, enabling batch training on large datasets in a simple but pragmatic sequential approach
The project is actively collecting user stories because understanding who uses Dask has been difficult due to unclear permission around sharing user information
Dask needs more help with spreading the word than it does with technical solutions
HPC deployment configuration is being overhauled to improve onboarding, which will cause breaking changes for existing users
money_quotes: Dask developers often get asked 'How do I deploy Dask on my Hadoop/Spark/Hive cluster?'. We haven't had a very good answer until recently.
Unfortunately Yarn has really only been accessible through a Java API, and so has been difficult for Dask to interact with.
If you ever wanted direct help on your cluster, now is the right time because Jim is working on this actively and is not yet drowned in user requests.
From a parallel computing perspective this is a very simple and un-sexy way of doing things. However my understanding is that it's also quite pragmatic.

In a distributed context we leave a lot of possible computation on the table (the solution is inherently sequential) but it's fun to see the model jump around the cluster as it absorbs various chunks of data.
Even though we're inundated with thousands of requests for help from various companies and research groups, it's never fully clear who minds having their information shared with others.
Arguably Dask needs more help with spreading the word than it does with technical solutions.
posts.key_insight: Open source projects often need as much help with community building and documentation as they do with technical development, and pragmatic solutions that sacrifice theoretical optimality for usability can be more valuable than elegant but complex alternatives.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin introduces dask-stories, a documentation site where Dask users can share how they use the project in practice. The initiative addresses common questions about Dask's real-world applications, scale, and industry adoption. Six initial users contributed stories spanning civic modeling, genomics, banking, cosmic ray detection, earth science, and hydrologic modeling. The post emphasizes that user stories are currently more valuable than code contributions because Dask is technically mature but not yet well-known enough.
key_points: New users learn best from existing users' experiences, making user stories a valuable form of documentation
The dask-stories project collects real-world use cases with a structured template covering problems, benefits, and pain points
Initial stories span diverse domains: civic modeling, genomics, banking, physics, earth science, and hydrology
User experience contributions may be more valuable than code at this stage of Dask's maturity
The model of collecting user stories could be adopted by other open source projects
money_quotes: New users can learn a lot from existing users.
Sharing real-world experiences like this are probably more valuable than code contributions to the Dask project at this stage.
Dask is more technically mature than it is well-known.
Users look to other users to help them understand a project.
If you use Dask today in an interesting way then please share your story. The world would love to hear your voice.
posts.key_insight: For mature open source projects, collecting and sharing user stories can be more valuable than additional code contributions because adoption depends on potential users seeing themselves in existing use cases.

posts.summary: Matt Rocklin provides a development update from SciPy 2018, highlighting several Dask-related projects and discussions that emerged from the conference. The dask-jobqueue project for running Dask on HPC systems was particularly well-received, with configurations established for major supercomputers. Multiple integration efforts are underway including TPOT for automated machine learning, scikit-optimize for hyperparameter tuning, and UMAP for dimensionality reduction. The post also announces efforts to centralize PyData/Scipy tutorials on Binder and soft-launches Dask Stories for collecting user experiences.
key_points: The dask-jobqueue project enables Dask deployment on traditional HPC job schedulers (PBS, SGE, SLURM, etc.) and was a major hit at the conference
Multiple machine learning integrations are in progress: TPOT for automated ML, scikit-optimize for smarter hyperparameter search, and UMAP for dimensionality reduction
A new initiative aims to centralize PyData/Scipy tutorials on Binder with embedded YouTube videos to improve discoverability of educational content
Rocklin praises the competing Ray project and suggests Dask will adopt ideas for actors/stateful operations while hoping Ray adopts standard Python interfaces
Dask Stories was soft-launched to collect real-world usage stories from the community
money_quotes: These are very common among scientific, research, and high performance machine learning groups but commonly a bit hard to use with anything other than MPI.
I think that by composing with Binder Min effectively just created the free-to-use hosted Bokeh server service.
However, this content isn't very discoverable after the conference.
We're probably not getting as much value out of these resources as we could be.
I suspect that Dask will steal ideas for actors for stateful operation. I hope that Ray takes on ideas for using standard Python interfaces so that more of the community can adopt it more quickly.
Dask-ML is a young and fast-moving project with many opportunities for new developers to get involved.
Having people like yourself share how they use Dask is incredibly important for the project.
posts.key_insight: Conference gatherings catalyze open source development not through completed work but by sparking collaborations, identifying integration opportunities, and building community around shared infrastructure challenges.

posts.summary: The post addresses a performance issue where Dask with PyTorch was slow due to inefficient pickle serialization. Rather than adding a Dask-specific workaround, the author contributed a five-line fix upstream to PyTorch that improved serialization from 1-50 MB/s to 1 GB/s. The key insight is that pickle itself isn't slow—it's a protocol that developers implement, and poor implementations create the perception of slowness. The author argues that supporting standard protocols like pickle benefits the entire Python ecosystem, allowing interoperability between Dask, PySpark, Ray, and other distributed systems without special-case code.
key_points: A five-line fix to PyTorch's __reduce__ method improved pickle serialization from 1-50 MB/s to 1 GB/s by using torch.save instead of .tolist()
Pickle is a protocol, not an implementation—slowness comes from how developers implement __reduce__, not from pickle itself
Fixing issues upstream in protocols benefits the entire ecosystem rather than just one library
Specialized serialization methods have their place but create friction for users and make library interoperability harder
The belief that 'pickle is just slow' prevented investigation into the actual performance bottleneck
money_quotes: Pickle is not slow. Pickle is a protocol. We implement pickle. If it's slow then it is our fault, not Pickle's.
We didn't solve a Dask problem, we solved an ecosystem problem.
I suspect that the common belief of 'Pickle is just slow' stopped anyone else from investigating the poor performance here.

Other libraries that need to interact definitely won't read the documentation, and even if they did it's not sensible for every library to special case every other library's favorite method to turn their objects into bytes.
Ecosystems of libraries depend strongly on the presence of protocols and a strong consensus around implementing them consistently and efficiently.
Today we use a large and rapidly changing set of libraries. It's hard for users to become experts in all of them.
posts.key_insight: When a protocol seems slow, investigate your implementation rather than blaming the protocol—and fix issues upstream to benefit the entire ecosystem rather than adding special-case workarounds.

posts.summary: This development log provides a transparent update on two weeks of Dask development work across eight different areas. The major highlights include an experimental Actors framework for stateful processing (inspired by Ray), machine learning experiments combining incremental training with hyper-parameter selection, and new preprocessing transformers for dask-ml. Infrastructure improvements include statistical profiling of the scheduler's event loop, new releases of dask-yarn, LSF cluster support in dask-jobqueue, and ongoing test suite cleanup to address intermittent failures in distributed testing.
key_points: Dask is adding an experimental Actors framework that trades resilience and diagnostics for reduced latency and direct state mutation on workers
Combining incremental training with hyper-parameter selection allows training multiple models simultaneously, making better use of processing power
The team is building lower-level tooling that accepts user-defined policies for hyper-parameter optimization, enabling experimentation without writing Dask code
Statistical profiling revealed performance issues in the scheduler's event loop that traditional profilers couldn't catch due to coroutine confusion
Testing distributed systems is hard - intermittent test failures add friction and are expensive to track down
money_quotes: A task-based framework like Dask can be forced into this kind of workload using long-running-tasks, but it's an uncomfortable experience.
The idea to adopt Actors was shamelessly stolen from the Ray Project :)
This training is inherently sequential because the model only trains on one batch of data at a time. We're leaving a lot of processing power on the table.
This is an active field of research and so it's hard for a general project like Dask to pick and implement a single method that works well for everyone.
Profiling concurrent code is hard. Traditional profilers like CProfile become confused by passing control between all of the different coroutines.
Testing distributed systems is hard.
posts.key_insight: Building a general-purpose distributed computing framework requires constantly balancing trade-offs between flexibility and optimization, often by borrowing good ideas from other projects and building lower-level primitives that let users experiment rather than prescribing single solutions.

posts.summary: This post documents a collaborative sprint where Fabian Pedregosa and Matt Rocklin implemented the SAGA optimization algorithm for Dask arrays. They walked through a four-step process: starting with a Numpy/Numba implementation, refactoring it into pure functions operating on lists of arrays, applying dask.delayed decorators, and then diagnosing performance issues via the Dask dashboard. The collaboration revealed both the relative ease of adapting research algorithms to Dask and the learning curve around proper use of dask.delayed. A key discovery was that most execution time was spent on Numba compilation rather than actual computation, leading to an upstream fix.
key_points: Converting a research algorithm to work with Dask requires restructuring code into pure functions that don't modify inputs or rely on global state
The dask.delayed decorator is easy to start using but requires experience to use well—tracking which variables are delayed vs. not is crucial
Dashboard visualization is essential for diagnosing performance issues like excessive client-side computation (visible as white gaps between tasks)
Profiling revealed an unexpected bottleneck: Numba recompilation was consuming most of the execution time, not the actual algorithm
The entire implementation took only an afternoon, demonstrating that adapting algorithms to Dask is tractable for researchers who program well but lack Dask experience
money_quotes: Fabian is good example of a researcher who knows how to program well and how to design ML algorithms, but has no direct exposure to the Dask library.
Fabian did this at first in about five minutes and to our mutual surprise, things actually worked
However, they didn't work that well.
Dask delayed is trivial to start using, but does require some experience to use well.
It's important to keep track of which operations and variables are delayed and which aren't. There is some cost to mixing between them.
We're not getting any parallelism (this is just a sequential algorithm) but we don't have much dead space.
The tasks, which used to take seconds, now take tens of milliseconds, so we can process through many more chunks in the same amount of time.
posts.key_insight: Adapting research algorithms to Dask is surprisingly tractable when you follow a disciplined process of refactoring into pure functions, but mastering dask.delayed requires learning to diagnose performance issues through dashboard visualization and profiling.

posts.summary: Cloud computing offers significant benefits but also risks vendor lock-in through proprietary services like Amazon Lambda, Google TPUs, and Azure Notebooks. While cloud vendors compete on genuinely beneficial features like cost and availability, they also create specialized services designed to make switching expensive. The antidote is adopting open standards like Kubernetes and Parquet, which force cloud providers to offer interoperable options and preserve users' freedom to migrate. The post urges public institutions to evaluate lock-in risks when choosing cloud services and encourages open source developers to focus on open standards rather than proprietary tooling.
key_points: Cloud computing adoption is accelerating across enterprises and public institutions due to flexibility, low upfront costs, and collaboration benefits
Cloud vendors intentionally create proprietary services that lock users in, making future migration expensive—a standard but problematic business practice
Open standards like Kubernetes and Parquet counter lock-in by forcing cloud providers to support interoperable technologies
Public institutions should mindfully evaluate cloud services and develop exit strategies before committing to proprietary platforms
Open source developers have a responsibility to invest in open standards rather than building around proprietary tooling
money_quotes: This stickiness to your current cloud 'locks you in' to using that particular cloud, even if policies change in the future in ways that you dislike.
This is OK, lock-in is a standard business practice. We shouldn't fault these commercial companies for making good business decisions.

If these open standard technologies become popular enough then cloud platforms must offer them alongside their proprietary technologies in order to stay competitive, removing one of their options for lock-in.

Technologies like Kubernetes and Parquet displace proprietary technologies like Amazon's Elastic Container Service (ECS), which locks users into AWS, or Google's BigQuery which keeps users on GCP with data gravity.
They've been pushed up the stack a bit, away from technologies that are infrastructural and towards technologies that are more about convenience and high-level usability.
Wide adoption of open standard infrastructure protects us from the control of cloud vendors.
By focusing our effort on open standards we provide public institutions with viable options for a safe cloud.
posts.key_insight: Open standards like Kubernetes and Parquet are the strategic counterweight to cloud vendor lock-in, pushing proprietary competition up the stack while preserving users' freedom to migrate.

posts.summary: As general-purpose open source software displaces domain-specific solutions, public institutions like NASA and NSF face difficult questions about how to serve their users while depending on externally-developed software. Rocklin examines common approaches that fail—like simply open-sourcing existing code or building new frameworks—and contrasts them with strategies that succeed, such as creating transition materials, contributing upstream, and enabling employees to maintain external projects. The key insight is that institutions must shift from leading development to playing a support role in larger ecosystems. Success requires patience, relationship-building with external communities, and recognizing that large public institutions have unique value in their long time horizons and public mandates.
key_points: Simply open-sourcing domain-specific software rarely attracts broad community engagement because the software is too specialized and maintaining open development is harder than expected
Pulling open source tools inside without external engagement leads to internal forks that diverge from upstream and create maintenance burdens
The most effective approach is developing learning materials and helping users transition before attempting to contribute code upstream
Institutions should explicitly allow employees to maintain upstream projects during work hours and count it toward career advancement
Co-writing grant proposals with OSS maintainers leverages institutional access to domain-specific funding channels that individual maintainers lack
money_quotes: Even very good software usually fails to attract a community.
It's very tempting to jump directly to this step and create new software, but waiting a while and focusing on teaching can often yield more long-lasting results.
The institution transitions from leading development and being fully in charge of its own destiny to playing a support role in a larger ecosystem.
Large public institutions are some of the only organizations with the public mandate and long time horizon to maintain public software over decade-long time scales.
These people will often do this work on their own at home or on the side in personal time if you don't step in.
Everyone needs help getting to know each other, and your institution is uniquely positioned to facilitate this.
Your engineers can help to filter and translate these requests into technical terms appropriate for the upstream project.
posts.key_insight: Public institutions must transition from controlling their own software destiny to becoming skilled participants in external ecosystems, leveraging their unique long-term mandates and domain expertise to support rather than lead development.

posts.summary: This post addresses a frequently asked question about dataframe performance across Pandas, Dask, Spark, and Arrow. Rocklin argues that Pandas remains the right choice for small datasets and computations under 100ms, while parallel solutions like Dask or Spark offer linear speedups for simple operations on large data but require careful configuration. The choice between Spark and Dask often comes down to cultural preferences (JVM vs Python ecosystems) rather than raw performance, with Spark excelling at large SQL-style queries and Dask better for time series and complex computations. Arrow is clarified as a data interchange format rather than a Pandas replacement, though computational features are planned for the future.
key_points: Parallelism has overhead costs that make it counterproductive for computations under ~100ms
Simple operations (filtering, aggregating) scale linearly with cores, but complex operations like distributed joins may not
Many Pandas performance issues can be solved without parallelism through better encoding, file formats, and avoiding groupby.apply
Spark vs Dask is often decided by cultural preference (JVM vs Python) rather than performance differences
Arrow is infrastructure for moving data between systems, not a computational library (though that may change)
money_quotes: If you're doing small computations then Pandas is always the right choice. The administrative costs of parallelizing will outweigh any benefit.
You should not parallelize if your computations are taking less than, say, 100ms.
Many people looking to speed up Pandas don't need parallelism.
This is often decided more by cultural preferences (JVM vs Python, all-in-one-tool vs integration with other tools) than performance differences.
Spark is generally higher level and all-in-one while Dask is lower-level and focuses on integrating into other tools.
Arrow is not a replacement for Pandas. Today Arrow is useful to people building systems and not to analysts directly like Pandas.
For complex operations like distributed joins it's more complicated. You might get linear speedups like above, or you might even get slowdowns.
posts.key_insight: The right dataframe tool depends more on your ecosystem preferences and problem characteristics than on abstract performance comparisons—parallelism isn't always faster, and cultural fit often matters more than benchmarks.

posts.summary: This release announcement for Dask 0.19.0 highlights several significant improvements to the distributed computing framework, including new Hadoop/YARN deployment tools, an experimental Actors framework for stateful workloads, and substantial dashboard enhancements. The release drops Python 3.4 support while adding Python 3.7 compatibility. The post also showcases the growing Dask ecosystem through user stories from diverse domains including genomics, climate science, and financial services, demonstrating the library's real-world applicability. The extensive contributor list reflects a healthy open-source community actively improving stability and feature parity with NumPy and Pandas.
key_points: New YARN/Hadoop deployment tools (conda-pack, Skein, dask-yarn) enable Dask on enterprise clusters without requiring JVM knowledge
Actors framework borrowed from Ray Project allows direct state mutation on workers with reduced latency, trading resilience for performance
Dashboard improvements include saveable task stream plots, responsive CSS for different screen sizes, and JupyterLab integration
Dask Stories initiative collects real-world use cases spanning genomics, climate science, urban modeling, and telecommunications
Over 70 contributors across three repositories in a roughly 3-month period shows strong community momentum
money_quotes: The idea to adopt Actors was shamelessly stolen from the Ray Project :)

Some advanced workloads want to directly manage and mutate state on workers. A task-based framework like Dask can be forced into this kind of workload using long-running-tasks, but it's an uncomfortable experience.

A ton of work has happened over the past two months, but most of the changes are small and diffuse. Stability, feature parity with upstream libraries (like Numpy and Pandas), and performance have all significantly improved, but in ways that are difficult to condense into blogpost form.
These stories help people understand where Dask is and is not applicable, and provide useful context around how it gets used in practice.
This is very valuable for communicating performance issues within a team.
posts.key_insight: Dask's strength lies not just in its technical capabilities but in its growing ecosystem of deployment tools, documentation, and community-contributed use cases that make distributed computing accessible across diverse domains.
